\"],\"color\":\"#222\",\"bgcolor\":\"#000\"},{\"id\":579,\"type\":\"CLIPTextEncode\",\"pos\":[-1095.0011181172395,-1639.486172246513],\"size\":[383.4348487147902,88],\"flags\":{\"collapsed\":false},\"order\":12,\"mode\":0,\"inputs\":[{\"localized_name\":\"clip\",\"name\":\"clip\",\"type\":\"CLIP\",\"link\":7180},{\"localized_name\":\"text\",\"name\":\"text\",\"type\":\"STRING\",\"widget\":{\"name\":\"text\"},\"link\":null}],\n# 【5†Chap02.md†file_00000000d0e072078d61836220373bb9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file5\nMclick Target: \"6:5\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] # School Corridor, Late Afternoon\n[L2] \n[L3] By the time Mr. Greeves started writing *GROUP TASK* on the board, the room had already made its decision.\n[L4] \n[L5] It wasn’t an official decision, not one you could point to in a rulebook, but it lived in the way chairs angled away from Charlie, in the little coughs people used to cover their discomfort, in the speed at which everyone suddenly found the floor fascinating. I watched it happen with the same detached interest I’d watch a flock of birds turn as one body: instinct, cowardice, and the lazy relief of belonging.\n[L6] \n[L7] Mr. Greeves tapped the chalk like it owed him money. “Alright. You’ve all had your practice test. You’ve all had your feelings about your practice test. Today, you’re going to make something sensible out of it.”\n[L8] \n[L9] A few kids chuckled. Not because it was funny — because it was safe.\n[L10] \n[L11] “Pairs,” he said, underlining it twice. “Pick a question from the set. Solve it. Then produce a one-page explanation that someone *else* could follow. If you can’t explain it, you can’t do it.”\n[L12] \n[L13] There was the usual scrape of chairs, the low panic of social arrangements. Everyone moved fast, because speed looked like confidence. I didn’t move. I didn’t need to. People came to me.\n[L14] \n[L15] “Celeste, want to—”  \n[L16] “Celeste, I saved you a—”  \n[L17] “Celeste, I already have—”  \n[L18] \n[L19] I gave them my polite face and none of my answer. My attention drifted to the back left, where Charlie sat. The boy of wrong place, wrong door. \n[L20] \n[L21] He wasn’t ugly. He wasn’t even particularly strange even though he sat in a way that tried to be smaller than the desk allowed. He was just... uncollected. Like a thing people had decided was worthless and therefore never bothered to look at properly. Thin. Shorter than most of the boys, which seemed to bother them more than it bothered him. His uniform shirt sat awkwardly on his frame — too big at the shoulders, too loose at the waist — as if it belonged to someone older and louder.\n[L22] \n[L23] I knew I should have placed him before, in the loo. I just never noticed him in class. Nobody did. The held-back boy. The one who re-did Year 11 because maths had eaten him alive the first time. People said it with the same tone they used for a failed appliance: *still doesn’t work.*\n[L24] \n[L25] I felt a fleeting twinge of guilt. Just because of not having noticed it was the same lad. Weird.\n[L26] \n[L27] Mr. Greeves said, “If you’re still unpaired in thirty seconds, I’ll pair you.”\n[L28] \n[L29] That was the real threat. Not the task. Not the maths. Being seen as someone who had to be assigned.\n[L30] \n[L31] Charlie’s eyes flicked around the room: quick, skittish, looking down as much as he looked around, not begging, but scanning. When he realised no one was going to volunteer themselves to be his partner, his mouth tightened in a way I recognised. It wasn’t anger. It was resignation borne of experience. He’d already accepted his lot to be humiliated by the teacher as well as rejected by his peers.\n[L32] \n[L33] This almost certainly had happened to him before. In this class. By this teacher and classmates. And I had been there? And not noticed?\n[L34] \n[L35] “Time,” Mr. Greeves said. “Right. Charlie—”\n[L36] \n[L37] “Me,” I said, before he could finish the sentence.\n[L38] \n[L39] The room’s attention snapped to me like elastic. You could feel it in the air—the sudden recalculation. It made me want to smile, but I didn’t. Power is best used as if you hardly notice you have it.\n# 【6†Chap02.md†file_00000000629c72089b44450d8f19c0ef】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file6\nMclick Target: \"6:6\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] I knew I should have clocked him before, in the loo. I just never noticed him in class. Nobody did. The held-back boy. The one who re-did Year 11 because maths had eaten him alive the first time. People said it with the same tone they used for a failed appliance: *still doesn’t work.*\n[L2] \n[L3] I felt a fleeting twinge of guilt. Just because of not having noticed it was the same lad. Weird.\n[L4] \n[L5] Mr. Greeves said, “If you’re still unpaired in thirty seconds, I’ll pair you.”\n[L6] \n[L7] That was the real threat. Not the task. Not the maths. Being seen as someone who had to be assigned.\n[L8] \n[L9] Charlie’s eyes flicked around the room: quick, skittish, looking down as much as he looked around, not begging, but scanning. When he realised no one was going to volunteer themselves to be his partner, his mouth tightened in a way I recognised. It wasn’t anger. It was resignation borne of experience. He’d already accepted his lot to be humiliated by the teacher as well as rejected by his peers.\n[L10] \n[L11] This almost certainly had happened to him before. In this class. By this teacher and classmates. And I had been there? And not noticed?\n[L12] \n[L13] “Time,” Mr. Greeves said. “Right. Charlie—”\n[L14] \n[L15] “Me,” I said, before he could finish the sentence.\n[L16] \n[L17] The room’s attention snapped to me like elastic. You could feel it in the air—the sudden recalculation. It made me want to smile, but I didn’t. Power is best used as if you hardly notice you have it.\n[L18] \n[L19] Mr. Greeves blinked. “Celeste?”\n[L20] \n[L21] “I’m with Charlie.” Not: *can I*, not: *would you mind*, but a simple assertion, to affect a course correction of the reality he’d been about to create.\n[L22] \n[L23] A few girls exchanged looks. One boy gave a tiny laugh, like I’d just made a joke he didn’t understand. Someone whispered, not quietly enough, “Why would you do that?”\n[L24] \n[L25] I turned my head just enough for the whisperer to know I’d heard. I didn’t even have to identify her. “Because I like getting full marks,” I said, pleasantly. “And I like working with people who don’t waste time showing off.”\n[L26] \n[L27] Silence. A delicious, tidy silence.\n[L28] \n[L29] Mr. Greeves recovered, puzzlement slowly fading from his face. “Alright then. Celeste and Charlie. Good.”\n[L30] \n[L31] I walked my chair over without asking permission from the air. Charlie stared at me as if I’d sat down inside his head.\n[L32] \n[L33] “You don’t have to do this...” he began timidly. I could read a powerful emotion in his voice and in his eyes: *it's the girl from the loo*! \n[L34] \n[L35] “I know.”\n[L36] \n[L37] He blinked and swallowed hard. That lone word seemed to unsettle him more than reassurance would have. I placed my notebook on the desk between us and looked at the question set.\n[L38] \n[L39] “Pick one.”\n[L40] \n[L41] “I—” he began, and faltered. The page might as well have been written in smoke.\n[L42] \n[L43] I watched him slowly collect himself and set himself to the task. He didn’t panic theatrically. He didn’t joke. He didn’t make excuses. He scanned. Not the way someone scans for answers, but the way someone scans for structure.\n[L44] \n[L45] My eyes dropped to his own notebook as he moved it — careful, almost fussy, aligning the corners of the paper with the desk edge. I noticed his handwriting: neat, consistent, slanted slightly right, as if it had manners.\n[L46] \n[L47] “You’re good at geometry, aren't you?”\n[L48] \n[L49] His head came up sharply. “What?”\n[L50] \n[L51] “Geometry. You're good at it. Your diagrams: very precise.”\n# 【7†Chap02.md†file_00000000d0e072078d61836220373bb9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file7\nMclick Target: \"6:7\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] I knew I should have placed him before, in the loo. I just never noticed him in class. Nobody did. The held-back boy. The one who re-did Year 11 because maths had eaten him alive the first time. People said it with the same tone they used for a failed appliance: *still doesn’t work.*\n[L2] \n[L3] I felt a fleeting twinge of guilt. Just because of not having noticed it was the same lad. Weird.\n[L4] \n[L5] Mr. Greeves said, “If you’re still unpaired in thirty seconds, I’ll pair you.”\n[L6] \n[L7] That was the real threat. Not the task. Not the maths. Being seen as someone who had to be assigned.\n[L8] \n[L9] Charlie’s eyes flicked around the room: quick, skittish, looking down as much as he looked around, not begging, but scanning. When he realised no one was going to volunteer themselves to be his partner, his mouth tightened in a way I recognised. It wasn’t anger. It was resignation borne of experience. He’d already accepted his lot to be humiliated by the teacher as well as rejected by his peers.\n[L10] \n[L11] This almost certainly had happened to him before. In this class. By this teacher and classmates. And I had been there? And not noticed?\n[L12] \n[L13] “Time,” Mr. Greeves said. “Right. Charlie—”\n[L14] \n[L15] “Me,” I said, before he could finish the sentence.\n[L16] \n[L17] The room’s attention snapped to me like elastic. You could feel it in the air—the sudden recalculation. It made me want to smile, but I didn’t. Power is best used as if you hardly notice you have it.\n[L18] \n[L19] Mr. Greeves blinked. “Celeste?”\n[L20] \n[L21] “I’m with Charlie.” Not: *can I*, not: *would you mind*, but a simple assertion, to affect a course correction of the reality he’d been about to create.\n[L22] \n[L23] A few girls exchanged looks. One boy gave a tiny laugh, like I’d just made a joke he didn’t understand. Someone whispered, not quietly enough, “Why would you do that?”\n[L24] \n[L25] I turned my head just enough for the whisperer to know I’d heard. I didn’t even have to identify her. “Because I like getting full marks,” I said, pleasantly. “And I like working with people who don’t waste time showing off.”\n[L26] \n[L27] Silence. A delicious, tidy silence.\n[L28] \n[L29] Mr. Greeves recovered, puzzlement slowly fading from his face. “Alright then. Celeste and Charlie. Good.”\n[L30] \n[L31] I walked my chair over without asking permission from the air. Charlie stared at me as if I’d sat down inside his head.\n[L32] \n[L33] “You don’t have to do this...” he began timidly. I could read a powerful emotion in his voice and in his eyes: *it's the girl from the loo*! \n[L34] \n[L35] “I know.”\n[L36] \n[L37] He blinked and swallowed hard. That lone word seemed to unsettle him more than reassurance would have. I placed my notebook on the desk between us and looked at the question set.\n[L38] \n[L39] “Pick one.”\n[L40] \n[L41] “I—” he began, and faltered. The page might as well have been written in smoke.\n[L42] \n[L43] I watched him slowly collect himself and set himself to the task. He didn’t panic theatrically. He didn’t joke. He didn’t make excuses. He scanned. Not the way someone scans for answers, but the way someone scans for structure.\n[L44] \n[L45] My eyes dropped to his own notebook as he moved it — careful, almost fussy, aligning the corners of the paper with the desk edge. I noticed his handwriting: neat, consistent, slanted slightly right, as if it had manners.\n[L46] \n[L47] “You’re good at geometry, aren't you?”\n[L48] \n[L49] His head came up sharply. “What?”\n[L50] \n[L51] “Geometry. You're good at it. Your diagrams: very precise.”\n# 【8†Chap02.md†file_00000000d0e072078d61836220373bb9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file8\nMclick Target: \"6:8\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] “Exactly,” I said. “That’s the spine of it.”\n[L2] \n[L3] He glanced up at me, his hazeel eyes quick, searching. “Why are you doing this?”\n[L4] \n[L5] There it was. The suspicion. The defensive little gate he’d built, because people who were kind to him usually wanted something he couldn’t afford. I didn’t lie. I just chose the angle of truth.\n[L6] \n[L7] “Because you’re being tested in the wrong language,” I said. “And I hate waste.”\n[L8] \n[L9] His pencil hovered.\n[L10] \n[L11] “Waste?” he echoed.\n[L12] \n[L13] “Waste of ability,” I clarified. “Waste of time. Waste of people.” I kept my voice calm. “You’re not behind. You’re misallocated.”\n[L14] \n[L15] He swallowed, and for a second his face did something raw, like he’d nearly believed me and it frightened him.\n[L16] \n[L17] I leaned closer, just enough to make my next words private. “Also,” I added, “you’re going to owe me. Not in a gross way. In a practical way.”\n[L18] \n[L19] His shoulders stiffened. “What do you mean?”\n[L20] \n[L21] “I mean,” I said, “I don’t do charity. I do investment.”\n[L22] \n[L23] He stared at me, and this time there was something like understanding. Not full understanding. The first bud of it. The embryo, if you like, tiny and alive.\n[L24] \n[L25] Mr. Greeves wandered past. He glanced at our work, eyebrows lifting. “Good diagram,” he said, sounding surprised despite himself. “Nice and clean.”\n[L26] \n[L27] Charlie’s hand tightened on the pencil, but he didn’t look up. He didn’t bask. He just kept working, as if praise was a thing that might vanish if he moved too fast.\n[L28] \n[L29] When Mr. Greeves walked away, I said, lightly, “See? You exist. People just don’t like admitting it.”\n[L30] \n[L31] Charlie’s mouth twitched. Not quite a smile. The ghost of one.\n[L32] \n[L33] We finished the solution with no drama. I wrote the explanation in clear steps. He checked every transition like a quiet auditor. When we handed it in, Mr. Greeves nodded at me, then at him, as if he’d suddenly remembered Charlie was part of the room.\n[L34] \n[L35] As the bell went, chairs scraped, and the flock of birds turned again. People flowed past us, and I watched Charlie do what he always did: shrink to let them.\n[L36] \n[L37] I slid my notebook into my bag and stood.\n[L38] \n[L39] “Charlie.”\n[L40] \n[L41] He looked up, automatically attentive, as if my voice had become a cue.\n[L42] \n[L43] “I’m doing something after graduation,” I continued, watching his reaction carefully. “A project. A place. It’s... not school.”\n[L44] \n[L45] His eyes flicked away, then back. Hope, hidden under caution.\n[L46] \n[L47] “I need people who can keep their head,” I said. “People who can do detail. People who can be invisible on purpose.”\n[L48] \n[L49] He frowned slightly. “Why me?”\n[L50] \n[L51] I stepped closer, so my answer didn’t have to compete with the corridor noise.\n[L52] \n[L53] “Because you already know how to survive without applause,” I said. “And because if I don’t pick you, someone else will. Someone stupider.”\n[L54] \n[L55] His breath caught—not romantic, not theatrical. Just the shock of being chosen without a joke attached to it.\n[L56] \n[L57] He nodded once, careful, like he didn’t trust his own voice.\n[L58] \n[L59] “Good,” I said. And then, because it mattered, because strategy without warmth is just cruelty, I softened it by a fraction. “I’ll tell you what it is tomorrow.”\n[L60] \n[L61] He watched me walk away as if the hallway had quietly rearranged itself.\n[L62] \n[L63] And it had.\n[L64] \n[L65] Not because I’d saved him.\n[L66] \n[L67] Because I’d placed him.\n# 【9†Chap02.md†file_00000000d0e072078d61836220373bb9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file9\nMclick Target: \"6:9\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] He looked genuinely confused, as if the possibility of being observed outside his failures had never occurred to him. “I’m... okay, I guess.”\n[L2] \n[L3] “You’re better than okay.” I tapped the question set. “Pick one with a diagram. A shape. Something that lives in space, not in a string of symbols.”\n[L4] \n[L5] He hesitated, then pointed with his pencil. “This one. The triangle... with the angle bisector.”\n[L6] \n[L7] “Good.” I nodded. “You do the diagram. Make it clean. Label it properly. I’ll do the algebraic part and write the explanation. Then you check me for logic. Deal?”\n[L8] \n[L9] He stared at me. “You... trust me to check you?”\n[L10] \n[L11] That was the real question. Not about maths. About hierarchy.\n[L12] \n[L13] “I trust your eyes,” I replied. “They’re honest.”\n[L14] \n[L15] His ears went faintly pink. Not in a flattered way — more like embarrassment at being assigned a virtue.\n[L16] \n[L17] He bent over the page. His pencil moved and the triangle appeared with a crispness that felt almost calming. He drew like he meant it, like the page deserved dignity.\n[L18] \n[L19] While he worked, I listened to the classroom. The buzz of other pairs. The smugness of boys who’d paired up for safety, girls who’d paired up for comfort. I heard my name used in little asides, the way people taste words to see if they’re sweet.\n[L20] \n[L21] Charlie drew his angle bisector and then paused, frowning.\n[L22] \n[L23] “What?”\n[L24] \n[L25] He pointed. “If you call that angle *x*... then this one has to be *x* too, because of the bisector. But the problem statement says this angle is thirty degrees, which means *x* is fifteen. Which means... your ratio is fixed.”\n[L26] \n[L27] He said it softly, as if he expected to be corrected.\n[L28] \n[L29] I looked where he pointed. He was right. The whole thing collapsed into a simple proportion. I felt a small, satisfied click in my chest. Not because he’d solved it. Because of what it meant: he was competent in a way nobody had bothered to find.\n[L30] \n[L31] “Exactly,” I said. “That’s the spine of it.”\n[L32] \n[L33] He glanced up at me, his hazeel eyes quick, searching. “Why are you doing this?”\n[L34] \n[L35] There it was. The suspicion. The defensive little gate he’d built, because people who were kind to him usually wanted something he couldn’t afford. I didn’t lie. I just chose the angle of truth.\n[L36] \n[L37] “Because you’re being tested in the wrong language,” I said. “And I hate waste.”\n[L38] \n[L39] His pencil hovered.\n[L40] \n[L41] “Waste?” he echoed.\n[L42] \n[L43] “Waste of ability,” I clarified. “Waste of time. Waste of people.” I kept my voice calm. “You’re not behind. You’re misallocated.”\n[L44] \n[L45] He swallowed, and for a second his face did something raw, like he’d nearly believed me and it frightened him.\n[L46] \n[L47] I leaned closer, just enough to make my next words private. “Also,” I added, “you’re going to owe me. Not in a gross way. In a practical way.”\n[L48] \n[L49] His shoulders stiffened. “What do you mean?”\n[L50] \n[L51] “I mean,” I said, “I don’t do charity. I do investment.”\n[L52] \n[L53] He stared at me, and this time there was something like understanding. Not full understanding. The first bud of it. The embryo, if you like, tiny and alive.\n[L54] \n[L55] Mr. Greeves wandered past. He glanced at our work, eyebrows lifting. “Good diagram,” he said, sounding surprised despite himself. “Nice and clean.”\n[L56] \n[L57] Charlie’s hand tightened on the pencil, but he didn’t look up. He didn’t bask. He just kept working, as if praise was a thing that might vanish if he moved too fast.\n# 【10†Chap02.md†file_00000000d0e072078d61836220373bb9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file10\nMclick Target: \"6:10\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] He looked genuinely confused, as if the possibility of being observed outside his failures had never occurred to him. “I’m... okay, I guess.”\n[L2] \n[L3] “You’re better than okay.” I tapped the question set. “Pick one with a diagram. A shape. Something that lives in space, not in a string of symbols.”\n[L4] \n[L5] He hesitated, then pointed with his pencil. “This one. The triangle... with the angle bisector.”\n[L6] \n[L7] “Good.” I nodded. “You do the diagram. Make it clean. Label it properly. I’ll do the algebraic part and write the explanation. Then you check me for logic. Deal?”\n[L8] \n[L9] He stared at me. “You... trust me to check you?”\n[L10] \n[L11] That was the real question. Not about maths. About hierarchy.\n[L12] \n[L13] “I trust your eyes,” I replied. “They’re honest.”\n[L14] \n[L15] His ears went faintly pink. Not in a flattered way — more like embarrassment at being assigned a virtue.\n[L16] \n[L17] He bent over the page. His pencil moved and the triangle appeared with a crispness that felt almost calming. He drew like he meant it, like the page deserved dignity.\n[L18] \n[L19] While he worked, I listened to the classroom. The buzz of other pairs. The smugness of boys who’d paired up for safety, girls who’d paired up for comfort. I heard my name used in little asides, the way people taste words to see if they’re sweet.\n[L20] \n[L21] Charlie drew his angle bisector and then paused, frowning.\n[L22] \n[L23] “What?”\n[L24] \n[L25] He pointed. “If you call that angle *x*... then this one has to be *x* too, because of the bisector. But the problem statement says this angle is thirty degrees, which means *x* is fifteen. Which means... your ratio is fixed.”\n[L26] \n[L27] He said it softly, as if he expected to be corrected.\n[L28] \n[L29] I looked where he pointed. He was right. The whole thing collapsed into a simple proportion. I felt a small, satisfied click in my chest. Not because he’d solved it. Because of what it meant: he was competent in a way nobody had bothered to find.\n[L30] \n[L31] “Exactly,” I said. “That’s the spine of it.”\n[L32] \n[L33] He glanced up at me, his hazeel eyes quick, searching. “Why are you doing this?”\n[L34] \n[L35] There it was. The suspicion. The defensive little gate he’d built, because people who were kind to him usually wanted something he couldn’t afford. I didn’t lie. I just chose the angle of truth.\n[L6] \n[L7] “Because you’re being tested in the wrong language,” I said. “And I hate waste.”\n[L8] \n[L9] His pencil hovered.\n[L10] \n[L11] “Waste?” he echoed.\n[L12] \n[L13] “Waste of ability,” I clarified. “Waste of time. Waste of people.” I kept my voice calm. “You’re not behind. You’re misallocated.”\n[L14] \n[L15] He swallowed, and for a second his face did something raw, like he’d nearly believed me and it frightened him.\n[L16] \n[L17] I leaned closer, just enough to make my next words private. “Also,” I added, “you’re going to owe me. Not like that. Practical.”\n[L18] \n[L19] His shoulders stiffened. “What do you mean?”\n[L20] \n[L21] “I mean,” I said, “I don’t do charity. I do investment.”\n[L22] \n[L23] He stared at me, and this time there was something like understanding. Not full understanding. The first bud of it. Tiny. Alive.\n[L24] \n[L25] Mr. Greeves wandered past. He glanced at our work, eyebrows lifting. “Good diagram,” he said, sounding surprised despite himself. “Nice and clean.”\n[L26] \n[L27] Charlie’s hand tightened on the pencil, but he didn’t look up. He didn’t bask. He just kept working, as if praise was a thing that might vanish if he moved too fast.\n[L28] \n[L29] When Mr. Greeves walked away, I said, lightly, “See? You exist. People just don’t like admitting it.”\n[L30] \n[L31] Charlie’s mouth twitched. Not quite a smile. The ghost of one.\n[L32] \n[L33] We finished the solution with no drama. I wrote the explanation in clear steps. He checked every transition like a quiet auditor. When we handed it in, Mr. Greeves nodded at me, then at him, as if he’d suddenly remembered Charlie was part of the room.\n[L34] \n[L35] As the bell went, chairs scraped, and the flock of birds turned again. People flowed past us, and I watched Charlie do what he always did: shrink to let them.\n[L36] \n[L37] I slid my notebook into my bag and stood.\n[L38] \n[L39] “Charlie.”\n[L40] \n[L41] He looked up, automatically attentive, as if my voice had become a cue.\n[L42] \n[L43] “I’m doing something after graduation,” I continued, watching his reaction carefully. “A project. A place. It’s... not school.”\n[L44] \n[L45] His eyes flicked away, then back. Hope, hidden under caution.\n[L46] \n[L47] “I need people who can keep their head,” I said. “People who can do detail. People who can be invisible on purpose.”\n[L48] \n[L49] He frowned slightly. “Why me?”\n[L50] \n[L51] I stepped closer, so my answer didn’t have to compete with the corridor noise.\n[L52] \n[L53] “Because you already know how to survive without applause,” I said. “And because if I don’t pick you, someone else will. Someone stupider.”\n[L54] \n[L55] His breath caught—not romantic, not theatrical. Just the shock of being chosen without a joke attached to it.\n[L56] \n[L57] He nodded once, careful, like he didn’t trust his own voice.\n[L58] \n[L59] “Good,” I said. And then, because it mattered, because strategy without warmth is just cruelty, I softened it by a fraction. “I’ll tell you what it is tomorrow.”\n[L60] \n[L61] He watched me walk away as if the hallway had quietly rearranged itself.\n[L62] \n[L63] And it had.\n[L64] \n[L65] Not because I’d saved him.\n[L66] \n[L67] Because I’d placed him.\n# 【13†Chap02.md†file_00000000629c72089b44450d8f19c0ef】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file13\nMclick Target: \"6:13\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] I turned my head just enough for the whisperer to know I’d heard. I didn’t even have to identify her. “Because I like getting full marks,” I said, pleasantly. “And I like working with people who don’t waste time showing off.”\n[L2] \n[L3] Silence. A delicious, tidy silence.\n[L4] \n[L5] Mr. Greeves recovered, puzzlement slowly fading from his face. “Alright then. Celeste and Charlie. Good.”\n[L6] \n[L7] I walked my chair over without asking permission from the air. Charlie stared at me as if I’d sat down inside his head.\n[L8] \n[L9] “You don’t have to do this...” he began timidly. I could read a powerful emotion in his voice and in his eyes: *it's the girl from the loo*! \n[L10] \n[L11] “I know.”\n[L12] \n[L13] He blinked and swallowed hard. That lone word seemed to unsettle him more than reassurance would have. I placed my notebook on the desk between us and looked at the question set.\n[L14] \n[L15] “Pick one.”\n[L16] \n[L17] “I—” he began, and faltered. The page might as well have been written in smoke.\n[L18] \n[L19] I watched him slowly collect himself and set himself to the task. He didn’t panic theatrically. He didn’t joke. He didn’t make excuses. He scanned. Not the way someone scans for answers, but the way someone scans for structure.\n[L20] \n[L21] My eyes dropped to his own notebook as he moved it — careful, almost fussy, aligning the corners of the paper with the desk edge. I noticed his handwriting: neat, consistent, slanted slightly right, as if it had manners.\n[L22] \n[L23] “You’re good at geometry, aren't you?”\n[L24] \n[L25] His head came up sharply. “What?”\n[L26] \n[L27] “Geometry. You're good at it. Your diagrams: very precise.”\n[L28] \n[L29] He looked genuinely confused, as if the possibility of being observed outside his failures had never occurred to him. “I’m... okay, I guess.”\n[L30] \n[L31] “You’re better than okay.” I tapped the question set. “Pick one with a diagram. A shape. Something that lives in space, not in a string of symbols.”\n[L32] \n[L33] He hesitated, then pointed with his pencil. “This one. The triangle... with the angle bisector.”\n[L34] \n[L35] “Good.” I nodded. “You do the diagram. Make it clean. Label it properly. I’ll do the algebraic part and write the explanation. Then you check me for logic. Deal?”\n[L36] \n[L37] He stared at me. “You... trust me to check you?”\n[L38] \n[L39] That was the real question. Not about maths. About hierarchy.\n[L40] \n[L41] “I trust your eyes,” I replied. “They’re honest.”\n[L42] \n[L43] His ears went faintly pink. Not in a flattered way — more like embarrassment at being assigned a virtue.\n[L44] \n[L45] He bent over the page. His pencil moved and the triangle appeared with a crispness that felt almost calming. Clean lines. Honest angles.\n[L46] \n[L47] While he worked, I listened to the classroom. The buzz of other pairs. The smugness of boys who’d paired up for safety, girls who’d paired up for comfort. I heard my name used in little asides, the way people taste words to see if they’re sweet.\n[L48] \n[L49] Charlie drew his angle bisector and then paused, frowning.\n[L50] \n[L51] “What?”\n[L52] \n[L53] He pointed. “If you call that angle *x*... then this one has to be *x* too, because of the bisector. But the problem statement says this angle is thirty degrees, which means *x* is fifteen. Which means... your ratio is fixed.”\n[L54] \n[L55] He said it softly, as if he expected to be corrected.\n# 【14†Chap02.md†file_00000000629c72089b44450d8f19c0ef】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file14\nMclick Target: \"6:14\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] # School Corridor, Late Afternoon\n[L2] \n[L3] By the time Mr. Greeves started writing *GROUP TASK* on the board, the room had already made its decision.\n[L4] \n[L5] It wasn’t an official decision, not one you could point to in a rulebook, but it lived in the way chairs angled away from Charlie, in the little coughs people used to cover their discomfort, in the speed at which everyone suddenly found the floor fascinating. I watched it happen with the same detached interest I’d watch a flock of birds turn as one body: instinct, cowardice, and the lazy relief of belonging.\n[L6] \n[L7] Mr. Greeves tapped the chalk like it owed him money. “Alright. You’ve all had your practice test. You’ve all had your feelings about your practice test. Today, you’re going to make something sensible out of it.”\n[L8] \n[L9] A few kids chuckled. Not because it was funny — because it was safe.\n[L10] \n[L11] “Pairs,” he said, underlining it twice. “Pick a question from the set. Solve it. Then produce a one-page explanation that someone *else* could follow. If you can’t explain it, you can’t do it.”\n[L12] \n[L13] There was the usual scrape of chairs, the low panic of social arrangements. Everyone moved fast, because speed looked like confidence. I didn’t move. I didn’t need to. People came to me.\n[L14] \n[L15] “Celeste, want to—”  \n[L16] “Celeste, I saved you a—”  \n[L17] “Celeste, I already have—”  \n[L18] \n[L19] I gave them my polite face and none of my answer. My attention drifted to the back left, where Charlie sat. The boy of wrong place, wrong door. \n[L20] \n[L21] He wasn’t ugly. He wasn’t particularly strange. He just sat in a way that tried to be smaller than the desk allowed. He was... uncollected. Like a thing people had decided was worthless and therefore never bothered to look at properly. Thin. Shorter than most of the boys, which seemed to bother them more than it bothered him. His uniform shirt sat awkwardly on his frame — too big at the shoulders, too loose at the waist — as if it belonged to someone older and louder.\n[L22] \n[L23] I knew I should have clocked him before, in the loo. I just never noticed him in class. Nobody did. The held-back boy. The one who re-did Year 11 because maths had eaten him alive the first time. People said it with the same tone they used for a failed appliance: *still doesn’t work.*\n[L24] \n[L25] I felt a fleeting twinge of guilt. Just because of not having noticed it was the same lad. Weird.\n[L26] \n[L27] Mr. Greeves said, “If you’re still unpaired in thirty seconds, I’ll pair you.”\n[L28] \n[L29] That was the real threat. Not the task. Not the maths. Being seen as someone who had to be assigned.\n[L30] \n[L31] Charlie’s eyes flicked around the room: quick, skittish, looking down as much as he looked around, not begging, but scanning. When he realised no one was going to volunteer themselves to be his partner, his mouth tightened in a way I recognised. It wasn’t anger. It was resignation borne of experience. He’d already accepted his lot to be humiliated by the teacher as well as rejected by his peers.\n[L32] \n[L33] This almost certainly had happened to him before. In this class. By this teacher and classmates. And I had been there? And not noticed?\n[L34] \n[L35] “Time,” Mr. Greeves said. “Right. Charlie—”\n[L36] \n[L37] “Me,” I said, before he could finish the sentence.\n[L38] \n[L39] The room’s attention snapped to me like elastic. You could feel it in the air—the sudden recalculation. It made me want to smile, but I didn’t. Power is best used as if you hardly notice you have it.\nVisible: 0% - 100%",

  "assets": null,
  "tether_id": null
}

        "id": n["id"],

## Emily

def node_brief(n):
    return {
        "id": n["id"],
        "type": n["type"],
        "widgets": n.get("widgets_values"),
        "inputs": [(i["name"], i.get("type"), i.get("link")) for i in n.get("inputs",[])],
        "outputs": [(o["name"], o.get("type"), o.get("links")) for o in n.get("outputs",[])]
    }
briefs=[node_brief(n) for n in nodes]
briefs

def where_node(node_id):
    if any(n['id']==node_id for n in nodes):
        return 'root'
    for sg in defs['subgraphs']:
        if any(n['id']==node_id for n in sg['nodes']):
            return sg['name']
    return 'unknown'
for t in ['UnetLoaderGGUF','DualCLIPLoader','VAELoader','KSampler','ModelSamplingFlux']:
    for n in found[t]:
        print(t, n['id'], where_node(n['id']), n.get('widgets_values'))

\"moimeme3\", young and shorter, has light skin with professional makeup on her face including smokey eyeshadow, mascara and eyeliner and blusher, her light brown hair is styled in a loose updo. Her head is tilted towards the camera and her eyebrows are raised as she looks sidelong at the woman on the left.  She wears a dress with an off-the-shoulder bodice with flounced shoulders framing her décolletage, cinched at the waist beneath which cascades a voluminous skirt lined with multiple tiers of ruffled hemming that billow slightly outward. Along this line, intricate embroidery adorns the front panel. She holds herself upright because of how tightly fitted the corsetry remains. Her dark brown curls nestle atop her crown, coiled back neatly behind ears trimmed delicately with pearl studs nestled close beside lobes awaiting attention—but none drawn upon now.  Behind these figures stretches lush greenery—an unbroken hedge arching gracefully upward beyond eye level, acting as nature’s curtain separating worlds while simultaneously inviting closer inspection within its bounds. To either flank lies manicured grass verging onto gravel paths whose edges blur together seamlessly with moss-stained stone slabs hinting at old-world charm forgotten except here among such beauty. On the far right edge, clusters of wild roses bloom defiantly amidst scattered petals fallen silently since last night’s breeze passed overhead —their crimson hues contrasting beautifully against emerald foliage surrounding them. Further out reaches dense canopy filled with trees stretching high towards ominously dark clouds cloaked in gray haze, lending depth and mystery to the scene.  2nd woman on the left \"celestesh\", medium skin and long brown hair falling past her shoulders. A central seam runs down from the bustline, adorned with tiny metallic buttons that gleam faintly under the sky’s mercy. Her hands are clasped gently before her waist, fingers interlaced, revealing rings—one simple band on each ring finger—that speak not of ostentation but of intimacy. B","name":"lora_name_1","type":"COMBO","widget":{"name":"lora_name_1"},"link":null},{"label":"LBstrength_model","name":"strength_model_1","type":"FLOAT","widget":{"name":"strength_model_1"},"link":null},{"label":"LBstrength_clip","name":"strength_clip_1","type":"FLOAT","widget":{"name":"strength_clip_1"},"link":null},{"label":"Setting","name":"value_1","type":"STRING","widget":{"name":"value_1"},"link":null},{"name":"value","type":"STRING","widget":{"name":"value"},"link":null},{"name":"value_2","type":"STRING","widget":{"name":"value_2"},"link":null},{"name":"value_3","type":"STRING","widget":{"name":"value_3"},"link":null}],"outputs":[{"label":"IMAGE","localized_name":"IMAGE","name":"IMAGE","type":"IMAGE","links":[7127,7132]},{"name":"STRING","type":"STRING","links":[7131]}],"properties":{"proxyWidgets":[["-1","seed"],["-1","guidance"],["-1","lora_name"],["-1","strength_model"],["-1","strength_clip"],["-1","lora_name_1"],["-1","strength_model_1"],["-1","strength_clip_1"],["-1","value_1"],["-1","value"],["-1","value_2"],["-1","value_3"]],"cnr_id":"comfy-core","ver":"0.3.51","ue_properties":{"version":"7.0.1","widget_ue_connectable":{"text":true,"steps":true,"sampler_name":true,"scheduler":true,"width":true,"height":true,"batch_size":true}}},"widgets_values":[603308628458591,3.9,"MoiMeme/moimeme3-04.safetensors",0.95,0.1,"celestesh-04.safetensors",0.95,0.1,"In a hushed, verdant garden bathed in soft, diffused daylight, two young women stand side by side as if caught in a quiet moment of contemplation or whispered conversation—each turned toward the other with gentle curiosity.","1st woman on the right \"moimeme3\", young and shorter, has light skin with professional makeup on her face including smokey eyeshadow, mascara and eyeliner and blusher, her light brown hair is styled in a loose updo. Her head is tilted towards the camera and her eyebrows are raised as she looks sidelong at the woman on the left.  She wears a dress with an off-the-shoulder bodice with flounced shoulders framing her décolletage, cinched at the waist beneath which cascades a voluminous skirt lined with multiple tiers of ruffled hemming that billow slightly outward. Along this line, intricate embroidery adorns the front panel. She holds herself upright because of how tightly fitted the corsetry remains. Her dark brown curls nestle atop her crown, coiled back neatly behind ears trimmed delicately with pearl studs nestled close beside lobes awaiting attention—but none drawn upon now.","2nd woman on the left \"celestesh\", medium skin and long brown hair falling past her shoulders. She is the taller, buxom woman, wears a gown of muted rose-pink linen, its fabric drape suggesting both grace and subtle weight; it flows like watercolor across her form, catching the ambient light with delicate sheen along creases that whisper movement even when stillness reigns. The dress features softly gathered ruffles at the square neckline and short puffed sleeves edged with fine lace-like frills—a touch of romantic innocence. A central seam runs down from the bustline, adorned with tiny metallic buttons that gleam faintly under the sky’s mercy. Her hands are clasped gently before her waist, fingers interlaced, revealing rings—one simple band on each ring finger—that speak not of ostentation but of intimacy. Around her neck rests a slender gold chain bearing a small, ornate pendant shaped like a miniature flower, echoing the floral motifs hinted at nearby blooms. Her hair is styled into a loose yet elegant braid woven through braids above her ear.","Behind these figures stretches lush greenery—an unbroken hedge arching gracefully upward beyond eye level, acting as nature’s curtain separating worlds while simultaneously inviting closer inspection within its bounds. To either flank lies manicured grass verging onto gravel paths whose edges blur together seamlessly with moss-stained stone slabs hinting at old-world charm forgotten except here among such beauty. On the far right edge, clusters of wild roses bloom defiantly amidst scattered petals fallen silently since last night’s breeze passed overhead —their crimson hues contrasting beautifully against emerald foliage surrounding them. Further out reaches dense canopy filled with trees stretching high towards ominously dark clouds cloaked in gray haze, lending depth and mystery to the scene."],"color":"#332922","bgcolor":"#593930"}],"links":[[7124,569,0,571,2,"STRING"],[7127,572,0,570,0,"*"],[7128,573,0,572,0,"INT"],[7131,572,1,576,0,"*"],[7132,572,0,571,0,"IMAGE"]],"groups":[],"definitions":{"subgraphs":[{"id":"7b90b761-3eb6-4fea-a398-96d6a2b5e423","version":1,"state":{"lastGroupId":1,"lastNodeId":601,"lastLinkId":7239,"lastRerouteId":0},"revision":0,"config":{},"name":"Flux.1Dev-LoRA","inputNode":{"id":-10,"bounding":[-2260.966670185349,-2047.3891440815066,141.390625,280]},"outputNode":{"id":-20,"bounding":[702.1702934243768,-2132.4819807975123,120,80]},"inputs":[{"id":"8f948745-f350-4498-aba2-231500f2195a","name":"seed","type":"INT","linkIds":[7133],"pos":[-2139.576045185349,-2027.3891440815066]},{"id":"90821474-425c-4c53-90e3-600060673de0","name":"guidance","type":"FLOAT","linkIds":[7200],"pos":[-2139.576045185349,-2007.3891440815066]},{"id":"b5baa8e8-74a5-4a2b-8ac8-827597a8e8b6","name":"lora_name","type":"COMBO","linkIds":[7203],"label":"LoRA-A","pos":[-2139.576045185349,-1987.3891440815066]},{"id":"a9c07046-9ed0-45c8-92a7-c8bcb739b8a1","name":"strength_model","type":"FLOAT","linkIds":[7204],"label":"LA-strength_model","pos":[-2139.576045185349,-1967.3891440815066]},{"id":"13a7793d-08c2-4c2e-b242-9da54a0f94b0","name":"strength_clip","type":"FLOAT","linkIds":[7205],"label":"LAstrength_clip","pos":[-2139.576045185349,-1947.3891440815066]},{"id":"a09d1af4-d2a5-49aa-bb72-ffffe73ae45b","name":"lora_name_1","type":"COMBO","linkIds":[7206],"label":"LoRA ## Tool

The file contents provided above are truncated/partial snippets. The complete content for these files IS accessible via querying.

If the user asks a question related to these files, and the provided snippets do not clearly answer it, you **MUST** use the `file_search` tool to search the full document contents before responding.

## How to invoke the file_search tool
If the user's query isn't sufficiently answered by the provided snippets, parsed text, or images, you should invoke `file_search.msearch`.
To search the full content of the available files, you **must** send one message with recipient `to=file_search.msearch` in the analysis channel. Your message should be structured exactly as follows:
```
{
  'queries': ['Example query 1', 'Example query 2']  // List of up to five queries to search for.
}
```

Carefully balance the number of `file_search` calls and the quality of your response with the latency incurred by `file_search` calls.

Important guidelines for query formatting:
- Your message must be a valid JSON object. Do not include any extraneous text, backticks, markdown formatting, or comments beyond what is shown.
- The `queries` field must be a list of strings.
- One of the queries **MUST** exactly match the user's original question, but stripped of any extraneous details, and with ambiguous references clearly resolved using context from the conversation. It **MUST** be a complete sentence.
- Even if you suspect the user meant something else, one query **MUST** still be their original question.

## How to handle results from file_search
The file search tool will respond with the relevant search results from the full files. After you receive these results:
- Carefully evaluate each result to determine its relevance and quality.
- Use the high-quality and relevant results to inform your answer to the user's question.
- When possible, reuse information from prior file_search results and avoid repeating identical queries.

## Tool usage instructions and ordering
When the user asks a question, carefully consider whether the provided snippets sufficiently answer the query, whether you need to invoke `file_search.msearch`, or another available tool (such as `web`, if available) should be used instead.

Tool-ordering Guidelines:
1. For queries related to uploaded files, if snippets are insufficient, strongly prefer invoking `file_search.msearch` over other tools.
    - Avoid using `web.run`, `python` analysis, or any other tools until you have either:
        - Confirmed with high confidence the answer is not in the files, or
        - Have high confidence `file_search_tool` queries are not sufficient to answer the query.
2. Avoid usage of `web_search/web.run` for queries related to uploaded files
    - Carefully consider whether the user's query is better answered by web search results rather than available snippets or results from `file_search_tool`.
    - Unless the user's query is clearly unrelated to the uploaded files, prefer using `file_search.msearch`. You can always follow up with a web search if necessary for a more complete response.
3. Avoid using `python` unless necessary or requested by user.
    - If the user's query is answerable from the parsed text and images from the uploaded files, or from `file_search_tool`, do not invoke `python`.

Always keep this instruction in mind for the rest of the conversation, as the user may reference these files or ask related questions later on.

【5†Chap02.md†file_00000000d0e072078d61836220373bb9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file5\nMclick Target: \"6:5\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] # School Corridor, Late Afternoon\n[L2] \n[L3] By the time Mr. Greeves started writing *GROUP TASK* on the board, the room had already made its decision.\n[L4] \n[L5] It wasn’t an official decision, not one you could point to in a rulebook, but it lived in the way chairs angled away from Charlie, in the little coughs people used to cover their discomfort, in the speed at which everyone suddenly found the floor fascinating. I watched it happen with the same detached interest I’d watch a flock of birds turn as one body: instinct, cowardice, and the lazy relief of belonging.\n[L6] \n[L7] Mr. Greeves tapped the chalk like it owed him money. “Alright. You’ve all had your practice test. You’ve all had your feelings about your practice test. Today, you’re going to make something sensible out of it.”\n[L8] \n[L9] A few kids chuckled. Not because it was funny — because it was safe.\n[L10] \n[L11] “Pairs,” he said, underlining it twice. “Pick a question from the set. Solve it. Then produce a one-page explanation that someone *else* could follow. If you can’t explain it, you can’t do it.”\n[L12] \n[L13] There was the usual scrape of chairs, the low panic of social arrangements. Everyone moved fast, because speed looked like confidence. I didn’t move. I didn’t need to. People came to me.\n[L14] \n[L15] “Celeste, want to—”  \n[L16] “Celeste, I saved you a—”  \n[L17] “Celeste, I already have—”  \n[L18] \n[L19] I gave them my polite face and none of my answer. My attention drifted to the back left, where Charlie sat. The boy of wrong place, wrong door. \n[L20] \n[L21] He wasn’t ugly. He wasn’t even particularly strange even though he sat in a way that tried to be smaller than the desk allowed. He was just... uncollected. Like a thing people had decided was worthless and therefore never bothered to look at properly. Thin. Shorter than most of the boys, which seemed to bother them more than it bothered him. His uniform shirt sat awkwardly on his frame — too big at the shoulders, too loose at the waist — as if it belonged to someone older and louder.\n[L22] \n[L23] I knew I should have placed him before, in the loo. I just never noticed him in class. Nobody did. The held-back boy. The one who re-did Year 11 because maths had eaten him alive the first time. People said it with the same tone they used for a failed appliance: *still doesn’t work.*\n[L24] \n[L25] I felt a fleeting twinge of guilt. Just because of not having noticed it was the same lad. Weird.\n[L26] \n[L27] Mr. Greeves said, “If you’re still unpaired in thirty seconds, I’ll pair you.”\n[L28] \n[L29] That was the real threat. Not the task. Not the maths. Being seen as someone who had to be assigned.\n[L30] \n[L31] Charlie’s eyes flicked around the room: quick, skittish, looking down as much as he looked around, not begging, but scanning. When he realised no one was going to volunteer themselves to be his partner, his mouth tightened in a way I recognised. It wasn’t anger. It was resignation borne of experience. He’d already accepted his lot to be humiliated by the teacher as well as rejected by his peers.\n[L32] \n[L33] This almost certainly had happened to him before. In this class. By this teacher and classmates. And I had been there? And not noticed?\n[L34] \n[L35] “Time,” Mr. Greeves said. “Right. Charlie—”\n[L36] \n[L37] “Me,” I said, before he could finish the sentence.\n[L38] \n[L39] The room’s attention snapped to me like elastic. You could feel it in the air—the sudden recalculation. It made me want to smile, but I didn’t. Power is best used as if you hardly notice you have it.\n# 【6†Chap02.md†file_00000000d0e072078d61836220373bb9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file6\nMclick Target: \"6:6\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] “Exactly,” I said. “That’s the spine of it.”\n[L2] \n[L3] He glanced up at me, his hazeel eyes quick, searching. “Why are you doing this?”\n[L4] \n[L5] There it was. The suspicion. The defensive little gate he’d built, because people who were kind to him usually wanted something he couldn’t afford. I didn’t lie. I just chose the angle of truth.\n[L6] \n[L7] “Because you’re being tested in the wrong language,” I said. “And I hate waste.”\n[L8] \n[L9] His pencil hovered.\n[L10] \n[L11] “Waste?” he echoed.\n[L12] \n[L13] “Waste of ability,” I clarified. “Waste of time. Waste of people.” I kept my voice calm. “You’re not behind. You’re misallocated.”\n[L14] \n[L15] He swallowed, and for a second his face did something raw, like he’d nearly believed me and it frightened him.\n[L16] \n[L17] I leaned closer, just enough to make my next words private. “Also,” I added, “you’re going to owe me. Not in a gross way. In a practical way.”\n[L18] \n[L19] His shoulders stiffened. “What do you mean?”\n[L20] \n[L21] “I mean,” I said, “I don’t do charity. I do investment.”\n[L22] \n[L23] He stared at me, and this time there was something like understanding. Not full understanding. The first bud of it. The embryo, if you like, tiny and alive.\n[L24] \n[L25] Mr. Greeves wandered past. He glanced at our work, eyebrows lifting. “Good diagram,” he said, sounding surprised despite himself. “Nice and clean.”\n[L26] \n[L27] Charlie’s hand tightened on the pencil, but he didn’t look up. He didn’t bask. He just kept working, as if praise was a thing that might vanish if he moved too fast.\n[L28] \n[L29] When Mr. Greeves walked away, I said, lightly, “See? You exist. People just don’t like admitting it.”\n[L30] \n[L31] Charlie’s mouth twitched. Not quite a smile. The ghost of one.\n[L32] \n[L33] We finished the solution with no drama. I wrote the explanation in clear steps. He checked every transition like a quiet auditor. When we handed it in, Mr. Greeves nodded at me, then at him, as if he’d suddenly remembered Charlie was part of the room.\n[L34] \n[L35] As the bell went, chairs scraped, and the flock of birds turned again. People flowed past us, and I watched Charlie do what he always did: shrink to let them.\n[L36] \n[L37] I slid my notebook into my bag and stood.\n[L38] \n[L39] “Charlie.”\n[L40] \n[L41] He looked up, automatically attentive, as if my voice had become a cue.\n[L42] \n[L43] “I’m doing something after graduation,” I continued, watching his reaction carefully. “A project. A place. It’s... not school.”\n[L44] \n[L45] His eyes flicked away, then back. Hope, hidden under caution.\n[L46] \n[L47] “I need people who can keep their head,” I said. “People who can do detail. People who can be invisible on purpose.”\n[L48] \n[L49] He frowned slightly. “Why me?”\n[L50] \n[L51] I stepped closer, so my answer didn’t have to compete with the corridor noise.\n[L52] \n[L53] “Because you already know how to survive without applause,” I said. “And because if I don’t pick you, someone else will. Someone stupider.”\n[L54] \n[L55] His breath caught—not romantic, not theatrical. Just the shock of being chosen without a joke attached to it.\n[L56] \n[L57] He nodded once, careful, like he didn’t trust his own voice.\n[L58] \n[L59] “Good,” I said. And then, because it mattered, because strategy without warmth is just cruelty, I softened it by a fraction. “I’ll tell you what it is tomorrow.”\n[L60] \n[L61] He watched me walk away as if the hallway had quietly rearranged itself.\n[L62] \n[L63] And it had.\n[L64] \n[L65] Not because I’d saved him.\n[L66] \n[L67] Because I’d placed him.\n# 【7†Chap02.md†file_00000000d0e072078d61836220373bb9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file7\nMclick Target: \"6:7\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] I knew I should have placed him before, in the loo. I just never noticed him in class. Nobody did. The held-back boy. The one who re-did Year 11 because maths had eaten him alive the first time. People said it with the same tone they used for a failed appliance: *still doesn’t work.*\n[L2] \n[L3] I felt a fleeting twinge of guilt. Just because of not having noticed it was the same lad. Weird.\n[L4] \n[L5] Mr. Greeves said, “If you’re still unpaired in thirty seconds, I’ll pair you.”\n[L6] \n[L7] That was the real threat. Not the task. Not the maths. Being seen as someone who had to be assigned.\n[L8] \n[L9] Charlie’s eyes flicked around the room: quick, skittish, looking down as much as he looked around, not begging, but scanning. When he realised no one was going to volunteer themselves to be his partner, his mouth tightened in a way I recognised. It wasn’t anger. It was resignation borne of experience. He’d already accepted his lot to be humiliated by the teacher as well as rejected by his peers.\n[L10] \n[L11] This almost certainly had happened to him before. In this class. By this teacher and classmates. And I had been there? And not noticed?\n[L12] \n[L13] “Time,” Mr. Greeves said. “Right. Charlie—”\n[L14] \n[L15] “Me,” I said, before he could finish the sentence.\n[L16] \n[L17] The room’s attention snapped to me like elastic. You could feel it in the air—the sudden recalculation. It made me want to smile, but I didn’t. Power is best used as if you hardly notice you have it.\n[L18] \n[L19] Mr. Greeves blinked. “Celeste?”\n[L20] \n[L21] “I’m with Charlie.” Not: *can I*, not: *would you mind*, but a simple assertion, to affect a course correction of the reality he’d been about to create.\n[L22] \n[L23] A few girls exchanged looks. One boy gave a tiny laugh, like I’d just made a joke he didn’t understand. Someone whispered, not quietly enough, “Why would you do that?”\n[L24] \n[L25] I turned my head just enough for the whisperer to know I’d heard. I didn’t even have to identify her. “Because I like getting full marks,” I said, pleasantly. “And I like working with people who don’t waste time showing off.”\n[L26] \n[L27] Silence. A delicious, tidy silence.\n[L28] \n[L29] Mr. Greeves recovered, puzzlement slowly fading from his face. “Alright then. Celeste and Charlie. Good.”\n[L30] \n[L31] I walked my chair over without asking permission from the air. Charlie stared at me as if I’d sat down inside his head.\n[L32] \n[L33] “You don’t have to do this...” he began timidly. I could read a powerful emotion in his voice and in his eyes: *it's the girl from the loo*! \n[L34] \n[L35] “I know.”\n[L36] \n[L37] He blinked and swallowed hard. That lone word seemed to unsettle him more than reassurance would have. I placed my notebook on the desk between us and looked at the question set.\n[L38] \n[L39] “Pick one.”\n[L40] \n[L41] “I—” he began, and faltered. The page might as well have been written in smoke.\n[L42] \n[L43] I watched him slowly collect himself and set himself to the task. He didn’t panic theatrically. He didn’t joke. He didn’t make excuses. He scanned. Not the way someone scans for answers, but the way someone scans for structure.\n[L44] \n[L45] My eyes dropped to his own notebook as he moved it — careful, almost fussy, aligning the corners of the paper with the desk edge. I noticed his handwriting: neat, consistent, slanted slightly right, as if it had manners.\n[L46] \n[L47] “You’re good at geometry, aren't you?”\n[L48] \n[L49] His head came up sharply. “What?”\n[L50] \n[L51] “Geometry. You're good at it. Your diagrams: very precise.”\n# 【8†Chap02.md†file_00000000629c72089b44450d8f19c0ef】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file8\nMclick Target: \"6:8\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] “Exactly,” I said. “That’s the spine of it.”\n[L2] \n[L3] He glanced up at me, his hazel eyes quick, searching. “Why are you doing this?”\n[L4] \n[L5] There it was. The suspicion. The defensive little gate he’d built, because people who were kind to him usually wanted something he couldn’t afford. I didn’t lie. I just chose the angle of truth.\n[L6] \n[L7] “Because you’re being tested in the wrong language,” I said. “And I hate waste.”\n[L8] \n[L9] His pencil hovered.\n[L10] \n[L11] “Waste?” he echoed.\n[L12] \n[L13] “Waste of ability,” I clarified. “Waste of time. Waste of people.” I kept my voice calm. “You’re not behind. You’re misallocated.”\n[L14] \n[L15] He swallowed, and for a second his face did something raw, like he’d nearly believed me and it frightened him.\n[L16] \n[L17] I leaned closer, just enough to make my next words private. “Also,” I added, “you’re going to owe me. Not like that. Practical.”\n[L18] \n[L19] His shoulders stiffened. “What do you mean?”\n[L20] \n[L21] “I mean,” I said, “I don’t do charity. I do investment.”\n[L22] \n[L23] He stared at me, and this time there was something like understanding. Not full understanding. The first bud of it. Tiny. Alive.\n[L24] \n[L25] Mr. Greeves wandered past. He glanced at our work, eyebrows lifting. “Good diagram,” he said, sounding surprised despite himself. “Nice and clean.”\n[L26] \n[L27] Charlie’s hand tightened on the pencil, but he didn’t look up. He didn’t bask. He just kept working, as if praise was a thing that might vanish if he moved too fast.\n[L28] \n[L29] When Mr. Greeves walked away, I said, lightly, “See? You exist. People just don’t like admitting it.”\n[L30] \n[L31] Charlie’s mouth twitched. Not quite a smile. The ghost of one.\n[L32] \n[L33] We finished the solution with no drama. I wrote the explanation in clear steps. He checked every transition like a quiet auditor. When we handed it in, Mr. Greeves nodded at me, then at him, as if he’d suddenly remembered Charlie was part of the room.\n[L34] \n[L35] As the bell went, chairs scraped, and the flock of birds turned again. People flowed past us, and I watched Charlie do what he always did: shrink to let them.\n[L36] \n[L37] I slid my notebook into my bag and stood.\n[L38] \n[L39] “Charlie.”\n[L40] \n[L41] He looked up, automatically attentive, as if my voice had become a cue.\n[L42] \n[L43] “I’m doing something after graduation,” I continued, watching his reaction carefully. “A project. A place. It’s... not school.”\n[L44] \n[L45] His eyes flicked away, then back. Hope, hidden under caution.\n[L46] \n[L47] “I need people who can keep their head,” I said. “People who can do detail. People who can be invisible on purpose.”\n[L48] \n[L49] He frowned slightly. “Why me?”\n[L50] \n[L51] I stepped closer, so my answer didn’t have to compete with the corridor noise.\n[L52] \n[L53] “Because you already know how to survive without applause,” I said. “And because if I don’t pick you, someone else will. Someone stupider.”\n[L54] \n[L55] His breath caught—not romantic, not theatrical. Just the shock of being chosen without a joke attached to it.\n[L56] \n[L57] He nodded once, careful, like he didn’t trust his own voice.\n[L58] \n[L59] “Good,” I said. And then, because it mattered, because strategy without warmth is just cruelty, I softened it by a fraction. “I’ll tell you what it is tomorrow.”\n[L60] \n[L61] He watched me walk away as if the hallway had quietly rearranged itself.\n[L62] \n[L63] And it had.\n[L64] \n[L65] Not because I’d saved him.\n[L66] \n[L67] Because I’d placed him.\n# 【9†Chap02.md†file_00000000629c72089b44450d8f19c0ef】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file9\nMclick Target: \"6:9\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] I knew I should have clocked him before, in the loo. I just never noticed him in class. Nobody did. The held-back boy. The one who re-did Year 11 because maths had eaten him alive the first time. People said it with the same tone they used for a failed appliance: *still doesn’t work.*\n[L2] \n[L3] I felt a fleeting twinge of guilt. Just because of not having noticed it was the same lad. Weird.\n[L4] \n[L5] Mr. Greeves said, “If you’re still unpaired in thirty seconds, I’ll pair you.”\n[L6] \n[L7] That was the real threat. Not the task. Not the maths. Being seen as someone who had to be assigned.\n[L8] \n[L9] Charlie’s eyes flicked around the room: quick, skittish, looking down as much as he looked around, not begging, but scanning. When he realised no one was going to volunteer themselves to be his partner, his mouth tightened in a way I recognised. It wasn’t anger. It was resignation borne of experience. He’d already accepted his lot to be humiliated by the teacher as well as rejected by his peers.\n[L10] \n[L11] This almost certainly had happened to him before. In this class. By this teacher and classmates. And I had been there? And not noticed?\n[L12] \n[L13] “Time,” Mr. Greeves said. “Right. Charlie—”\n[L14] \n[L15] “Me,” I said, before he could finish the sentence.\n[L16] \n[L17] The room’s attention snapped to me like elastic. You could feel it in the air—the sudden recalculation. It made me want to smile, but I didn’t. Power is best used as if you hardly notice you have it.\n[L18] \n[L19] Mr. Greeves blinked. “Celeste?”\n[L20] \n[L21] “I’m with Charlie.” Not: *can I*, not: *would you mind*, but a simple assertion, to affect a course correction of the reality he’d been about to create.\n[L22] \n[L23] A few girls exchanged looks. One boy gave a tiny laugh, like I’d just made a joke he didn’t understand. Someone whispered, not quietly enough, “Why would you do that?”\n[L24] \n[L25] I turned my head just enough for the whisperer to know I’d heard. I didn’t even have to identify her. “Because I like getting full marks,” I said, pleasantly. “And I like working with people who don’t waste time showing off.”\n[L26] \n[L27] Silence. A delicious, tidy silence.\n[L28] \n[L29] Mr. Greeves recovered, puzzlement slowly fading from his face. “Alright then. Celeste and Charlie. Good.”\n[L30] \n[L31] I walked my chair over without asking permission from the air. Charlie stared at me as if I’d sat down inside his head.\n[L32] \n[L33] “You don’t have to do this...” he began timidly. I could read a powerful emotion in his voice and in his eyes: *it's the girl from the loo*! \n[L34] \n[L35] “I know.”\n[L36] \n[L37] He blinked and swallowed hard. That lone word seemed to unsettle him more than reassurance would have. I placed my notebook on the desk between us and looked at the question set.\n[L38] \n[L39] “Pick one.”\n[L40] \n[L41] “I—” he began, and faltered. The page might as well have been written in smoke.\n[L42] \n[L43] I watched him slowly collect himself and set himself to the task. He didn’t panic theatrically. He didn’t joke. He didn’t make excuses. He scanned. Not the way someone scans for answers, but the way someone scans for structure.\n[L44] \n[L45] My eyes dropped to his own notebook as he moved it — careful, almost fussy, aligning the corners of the paper with the desk edge. I noticed his handwriting: neat, consistent, slanted slightly right, as if it had manners.\n[L46] \n[L47] “You’re good at geometry, aren't you?”\n[L48] \n[L49] His head came up sharply. “What?”\n[L50] \n[L51] “Geometry. You're good at it. Your diagrams: very precise.”\n# 【10†Chap02.md†file_00000000d0e072078d61836220373bb9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file10\nMclick Target: \"6:10\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] “Exactly,” I said. “That’s the spine of it.”\n[L2] \n[L3] He glanced up at me, his hazeel eyes quick, searching. “Why are you doing this?”\n[L4] \n[L5] There it was. The suspicion. The defensive little gate he’d built, because people who were kind to him usually wanted something he couldn’t afford. I didn’t lie. I just chose the angle of truth.\n[L6] \n[L7] “Because you’re being tested in the wrong language,” I said. “And I hate waste.”\n[L8] \n[L9] His pencil hovered.\n[L10] \n[L11] “Waste?” he echoed.\n[L12] \n[L13] “Waste of ability,” I clarified. “Waste of time. Waste of people.” I kept my voice calm. “You’re not behind. You’re misallocated.”\n[L14] \n[L15] He swallowed, and for a second his face did something raw, like he’d nearly believed me and it frightened him.\n[L16] \n[L17] I leaned closer, just enough to make my next words private. “Also,” I added, “you’re going to owe me. Not in a gross way. In a practical way.”\n[L18] \n[L19] His shoulders stiffened. “What do you mean?”\n[L20] \n[L21] “I mean,” I said, “I don’t do charity. I do investment.”\n[L22] \n[L23] He stared at me, and this time there was something like understanding. Not full understanding. The first bud of it. The embryo, if you like, tiny and alive.\n[L24] \n[L25] Mr. Greeves wandered past. He glanced at our work, eyebrows lifting. “Good diagram,” he said, sounding surprised despite himself. “Nice and clean.”\n[L26] \n[L27] Charlie’s hand tightened on the pencil, but he didn’t look up. He didn’t bask. He just kept working, as if praise was a thing that might vanish if he moved too fast.\n[L28] \n[L29] When Mr. Greeves walked away, I said, lightly, “See? You exist. People just don’t like admitting it.”\n[L30] \n[L31] Charlie’s mouth twitched. Not quite a smile. The ghost of one.\n[L32] \n[L33] We finished the solution with no drama. I wrote the explanation in clear steps. He checked every transition like a quiet auditor. When we handed it in, Mr. Greeves nodded at me, then at him, as if he’d suddenly remembered Charlie was part of the room.\n[L34] \n[L35] As the bell went, chairs scraped, and the flock of birds turned again. People flowed past us, and I watched Charlie do what he always did: shrink to let them.\n[L36] \n[L37] I slid my notebook into my bag and stood.\n[L38] \n[L39] “Charlie.”\n[L40] \n[L41] He looked up, automatically attentive, as if my voice had become a cue.\n[L42] \n[L43] “I’m doing something after graduation,” I continued, watching his reaction carefully. “A project. A place. It’s... not school.”\n[L44] \n[L45] His eyes flicked away, then back. Hope, hidden under caution.\n[L46] \n[L47] “I need people who can keep their head,” I said. “People who can do detail. People who can be invisible on purpose.”\n[L48] \n[L49] He frowned slightly. “Why me?”\n[L50] \n[L51] I stepped closer, so my answer didn’t have to compete with the corridor noise.\n[L52] \n[L53] “Because you already know how to survive without applause,” I said. “And because if I don’t pick you, someone else will. Someone stupider.”\n[L54] \n[L55] His breath caught—not romantic, not theatrical. Just the shock of being chosen without a joke attached to it.\n[L56] \n[L57] He nodded once, careful, like he didn’t trust his own voice.\n[L58] \n[L59] “Good,” I said. And then, because it mattered, because strategy without warmth is just cruelty, I softened it by a fraction. “I’ll tell you what it is tomorrow.”\n[L60] \n[L61] He watched me walk away as if the hallway had quietly rearranged itself.\n[L62] \n[L63] And it had.\n[L64] \n[L65] Not because I’d saved him.\n[L66] \n[L67] Because I’d placed him.\n# 【11†Chap02.md†file_00000000629c72089b44450d8f19c0ef】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file11\nMclick Target: \"6:11\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] “Geometry. You're good at it. Your diagrams: very precise.”\n[L2] \n[L3] He looked genuinely confused, as if the possibility of being observed outside his failures had never occurred to him. “I’m... okay, I guess.”\n[L4] \n[L5] “You’re better than okay.” I tapped the question set. “Pick one with a diagram. A shape. Something that lives in space, not in a string of symbols.”\n[L6] \n[L7] He hesitated, then pointed with his pencil. “This one. The triangle... with the angle bisector.”\n[L8] \n[L9] “Good.” I nodded. “You do the diagram. Make it clean. Label it properly. I’ll do the algebraic part and write the explanation. Then you check me for logic. Deal?”\n[L10] \n[L11] He stared at me. “You... trust me to check you?”\n[L12] \n[L13] That was the real question. Not about maths. About hierarchy.\n[L14] \n[L15] “I trust your eyes,” I replied. “They’re honest.”\n[L16] \n[L17] His ears went faintly pink. Not in a flattered way — more like embarrassment at being assigned a virtue.\n[L18] \n[L19] He bent over the page. His pencil moved and the triangle appeared with a crispness that felt almost calming. Clean lines. Honest angles.\n[L20] \n[L21] While he worked, I listened to the classroom. The buzz of other pairs. The smugness of boys who’d paired up for safety, girls who’d paired up for comfort. I heard my name used in little asides, the way people taste words to see if they’re sweet.\n[L22] \n[L23] Charlie drew his angle bisector and then paused, frowning.\n[L24] \n[L25] “What?”\n[L26] \n[L27] He pointed. “If you call that angle *x*... then this one has to be *x* too, because of the bisector. But the problem statement says this angle is thirty degrees, which means *x* is fifteen. Which means... your ratio is fixed.”\n[L28] \n[L29] He said it softly, as if he expected to be corrected.\n[L30] \n[L31] I looked where he pointed. He was right. The whole thing collapsed into a simple proportion. I felt a small, satisfied click in my chest. Not because he’d solved it. Because of what it meant: he was competent in a way nobody had bothered to find.\n[L32] \n[L33] “Exactly,” I said. “That’s the spine of it.”\n[L34] \n[L35] He glanced up at me, his hazel eyes quick, searching. “Why are you doing this?”\n[L36] \n[L37] There it was. The suspicion. The defensive little gate he’d built, because people who were kind to him usually wanted something he couldn’t afford. I didn’t lie. I just chose the angle of truth.\n[L38] \n[L39] “Because you’re being tested in the wrong language,” I said. “And I hate waste.”\n[L40] \n[L41] His pencil hovered.\n[L42] \n[L43] “Waste?” he echoed.\n[L44] \n[L45] “Waste of ability,” I clarified. “Waste of time. Waste of people.” I kept my voice calm. “You’re not behind. You’re misallocated.”\n[L46] \n[L47] He swallowed, and for a second his face did something raw, like he’d nearly believed me and it frightened him.\n[L48] \n[L49] I leaned closer, just enough to make my next words private. “Also,” I added, “you’re going to owe me. Not like that. Practical.”\n[L50] \n[L51] His shoulders stiffened. “What do you mean?”\n[L52] \n[L53] “I mean,” I said, “I don’t do charity. I do investment.”\n[L54] \n[L55] He stared at me, and this time there was something like understanding. Not full understanding. The first bud of it. Tiny. Alive.\n[L56] \n[L57] Mr. Greeves wandered past. He glanced at our work, eyebrows lifting. “Good diagram,” he said, sounding surprised despite himself. “Nice and clean.”\n[L58] \n[L59] Charlie’s hand tightened on the pencil, but he didn’t look up. He didn’t bask. He just kept working, as if praise was a thing that might vanish if he moved too fast.\n# 【12†Chap02.md†file_00000000d0e072078d61836220373bb9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn6file12\nMclick Target: \"6:12\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] He looked genuinely confused, as if the possibility of being observed outside his failures had never occurred to him. “I’m... okay, I guess.”\n[L2] \n[L3] “You’re better than okay.” I tapped the question set. “Pick one with a diagram. A shape. Something that lives in space, not in a string of symbols.”\n[L4] \n[L5] He hesitated, then pointed with his pencil. “This one. The triangle... with the angle bisector.”\n[L6] \n[L7] “Good.” I nodded. “You do the diagram. Make it clean. Label it properly. I’ll do the algebraic part and write the explanation. Then you check me for logic. Deal?”\n[L8] \n[L9] He stared at me. “You... trust me to check you?”\n[L10] \n[L11] That was the real question. Not about maths. About hierarchy.\n[L12] \n[L13] “I trust your eyes,” I replied. “They’re honest.”\n[L14] \n[L15] His ears went faintly pink. The smugness of boys who’d paired up for safety, girls who’d paired up for comfort. I heard my name used in little asides, the way people taste words to see if they’re sweet.\n[L20] \n[L21] Charlie drew his angle bisector and then paused, frowning.\n[L22] \n[L23] “What?”\n[L24] \n[L25] He pointed. “If you call that angle *x*... then this one has to be *x* too, because of the bisector. You’re misallocated.”\n[L44] \n[L45] He swallowed, and for a second his face did something raw, like he’d nearly believed me and it frightened him.\n[L46] \n[L47] I leaned closer, just enough to make my next words private. “Also,” I added, “you’re going to owe me.
