【5†Chap02.md†file_00000000629c72089b44450d8f19c0ef】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn9file5\nMclick Target: \"9:5\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] I knew I should have clocked him before, in the loo. I just never noticed him in class. Nobody did. The held-back boy. The one who re-did Year 11 because maths had eaten him alive the first time. People said it with the same tone they used for a failed appliance: *still doesn’t work.*\n[L2] \n[L3] I felt a fleeting twinge of guilt. Just because of not having noticed it was the same lad. Weird.\n[L4] \n[L5] Mr. Greeves said, “If you’re still unpaired in thirty seconds, I’ll pair you.”\n[L6] \n[L7] That was the real threat. Not the task. Not the maths. Being seen as someone who had to be assigned.\n[L8] \n[L9] Charlie’s eyes flicked around the room: quick, skittish, looking down as much as he looked around, not begging, but scanning. When he realised no one was going to volunteer themselves to be his partner, his mouth tightened in a way I recognised. It wasn’t anger. It was resignation borne of experience. He’d already accepted his lot to be humiliated by the teacher as well as rejected by his peers.\n[L10] \n[L11] This almost certainly had happened to him before. In this class. By this teacher and classmates. And I had been there? And not noticed?\n[L12] \n[L13] “Time,” Mr. Greeves said. “Right. Charlie—”\n[L14] \n[L15] “Me,” I said, before he could finish the sentence.\n[L16] \n[L17] The room’s attention snapped to me like elastic. You could feel it in the air—the sudden recalculation. It made me want to smile, but I didn’t. Power is best used as if you hardly notice you have it.\n[L18] \n[L19] Mr. Greeves blinked. “Celeste?”\n[L20] \n[L21] “I’m with Charlie.” Not: *can I*, not: *would you mind*, but a simple assertion, to affect a course correction of the reality he’d been about to create.\n[L22] \n[L23] A few girls exchanged looks. One boy gave a tiny laugh, like I’d just made a joke he didn’t understand. Someone whispered, not quietly enough, “Why would you do that?”\n[L24] \n[L25] I turned my head just enough for the whisperer to know I’d heard. I didn’t even have to identify her. “Because I like getting full marks,” I said, pleasantly. “And I like working with people who don’t waste time showing off.”\n[L26] \n[L27] Silence. A delicious, tidy silence.\n[L28] \n[L29] Mr. Greeves recovered, puzzlement slowly fading from his face. “Alright then. Celeste and Charlie. Good.”\n[L30] \n[L31] I walked my chair over without asking permission from the air. Charlie stared at me as if I’d sat down inside his head.\n[L32] \n[L33] “You don’t have to do this...” he began timidly. I could read a powerful emotion in his voice and in his eyes: *it's the girl from the loo*! \n[L34] \n[L35] “I know.”\n[L36] \n[L37] He blinked and swallowed hard. That lone word seemed to unsettle him more than reassurance would have. I placed my notebook on the desk between us and looked at the question set.\n[L38] \n[L39] “Pick one.”\n[L40] \n[L41] “I—” he began, and faltered. The page might as well have been written in smoke.\n[L42] \n[L43] I watched him slowly collect himself and set himself to the task. He didn’t panic theatrically. He didn’t joke. He didn’t make excuses. He scanned. Not the way someone scans for answers, but the way someone scans for structure.\n[L44] \n[L45] My eyes dropped to his own notebook as he moved it — careful, almost fussy, aligning the corners of the paper with the desk edge. I noticed his handwriting: neat, consistent, slanted slightly right, as if it had manners.\n[L46] \n[L47] “You’re good at geometry, aren't you?”\n[L48] \n[L49] His head came up sharply. “What?”\n[L50] \n[L51] “Geometry. You're good at it. Your diagrams: very precise.”\n# 【6†Chap02.md†file_00000000629c72089b44450d8f19c0ef】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn9file6\nMclick Target: \"9:6\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] # School Corridor, Late Afternoon\n[L2] \n[L3] By the time Mr. Greeves started writing *GROUP TASK* on the board, the room had already made its decision.\n[L4] \n[L5] It wasn’t an official decision, not one you could point to in a rulebook, but it lived in the way chairs angled away from Charlie, in the little coughs people used to cover their discomfort, in the speed at which everyone suddenly found the floor fascinating. I watched it happen with the same detached interest I’d watch a flock of birds turn as one body: instinct, cowardice, and the lazy relief of belonging.\n[L6] \n[L7] Mr. Greeves tapped the chalk like it owed him money. “Alright. You’ve all had your practice test. You’ve all had your feelings about your practice test. Today, you’re going to make something sensible out of it.”\n[L8] \n[L9] A few kids chuckled. Not because it was funny — because it was safe.\n[L10] \n[L11] “Pairs,” he said, underlining it twice. “Pick a question from the set. Solve it. Then produce a one-page explanation that someone *else* could follow. If you can’t explain it, you can’t do it.”\n[L12] \n[L13] There was the usual scrape of chairs, the low panic of social arrangements. Everyone moved fast, because speed looked like confidence. I didn’t move. I didn’t need to. People came to me.\n[L14] \n[L15] “Celeste, want to—”  \n[L16] “Celeste, I saved you a—”  \n[L17] “Celeste, I already have—”  \n[L18] \n[L19] I gave them my polite face and none of my answer. My attention drifted to the back left, where Charlie sat. The boy of wrong place, wrong door. \n[L20] \n[L21] He wasn’t ugly. He wasn’t particularly strange. He just sat in a way that tried to be smaller than the desk allowed. He was... uncollected. Like a thing people had decided was worthless and therefore never bothered to look at properly. Thin. Shorter than most of the boys, which seemed to bother them more than it bothered him. His uniform shirt sat awkwardly on his frame — too big at the shoulders, too loose at the waist — as if it belonged to someone older and louder.\n[L22] \n[L23] I knew I should have clocked him before, in the loo. I just never noticed him in class. Nobody did. The held-back boy. The one who re-did Year 11 because maths had eaten him alive the first time. People said it with the same tone they used for a failed appliance: *still doesn’t work.*\n[L24] \n[L25] I felt a fleeting twinge of guilt. Just because of not having noticed it was the same lad. Weird.\n[L26] \n[L27] Mr. Greeves said, “If you’re still unpaired in thirty seconds, I’ll pair you.”\n[L28] \n[L29] That was the real threat. Not the task. Not the maths. Being seen as someone who had to be assigned.\n[L30] \n[L31] Charlie’s eyes flicked around the room: quick, skittish, looking down as much as he looked around, not begging, but scanning. When he realised no one was going to volunteer themselves to be his partner, his mouth tightened in a way I recognised. It wasn’t anger. It was resignation borne of experience. He’d already accepted his lot to be humiliated by the teacher as well as rejected by his peers.\n[L32] \n[L33] This almost certainly had happened to him before. In this class. By this teacher and classmates. And I had been there? And not noticed?\n[L34] \n[L35] “Time,” Mr. Greeves said. “Right. Charlie—”\n[L36] \n[L37] “Me,” I said, before he could finish the sentence.\n[L38] \n[L39] The room’s attention snapped to me like elastic. You could feel it in the air—the sudden recalculation. It made me want to smile, but I didn’t. Power is best used as if you hardly notice you have it.\n# 【7†Chap02.md†file_00000000d0e072078d61836220373bb9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn9file7\nMclick Target: \"9:7\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] # School Corridor, Late Afternoon\n[L2] \n[L3] By the time Mr. Greeves started writing *GROUP TASK* on the board, the room had already made its decision.\n[L4] \n[L5] It wasn’t an official decision, not one you could point to in a rulebook, but it lived in the way chairs angled away from Charlie, in the little coughs people used to cover their discomfort, in the speed at which everyone suddenly found the floor fascinating. I watched it happen with the same detached interest I’d watch a flock of birds turn as one body: instinct, cowardice, and the lazy relief of belonging.\n[L6] \n[L7] Mr. Greeves tapped the chalk like it owed him money. “Alright. You’ve all had your practice test. You’ve all had your feelings about your practice test. Today, you’re going to make something sensible out of it.”\n[L8] \n[L9] A few kids chuckled. Not because it was funny — because it was safe.\n[L10] \n[L11] “Pairs,” he said, underlining it twice. “Pick a question from the set. Solve it. Then produce a one-page explanation that someone *else* could follow. If you can’t explain it, you can’t do it.”\n[L12] \n[L13] There was the usual scrape of chairs, the low panic of social arrangements. Everyone moved fast, because speed looked like confidence. I didn’t move. I didn’t need to. People came to me.\n[L14] \n[L15] “Celeste, want to—”  \n[L16] “Celeste, I saved you a—”  \n[L17] “Celeste, I already have—”  \n[L18] \n[L19] I gave them my polite face and none of my answer. My attention drifted to the back left, where Charlie sat. The boy of wrong place, wrong door. \n[L20] \n[L21] He wasn’t ugly. He wasn’t even particularly strange even though he sat in a way that tried to be smaller than the desk allowed. He was just... uncollected. Like a thing people had decided was worthless and therefore never bothered to look at properly. Thin. Shorter than most of the boys, which seemed to bother them more than it bothered him. His uniform shirt sat awkwardly on his frame — too big at the shoulders, too loose at the waist — as if it belonged to someone older and louder.\n[L22] \n[L23] I knew I should have placed him before, in the loo. I just never noticed him in class. Nobody did. The held-back boy. The one who re-did Year 11 because maths had eaten him alive the first time. People said it with the same tone they used for a failed appliance: *still doesn’t work.*\n[L24] \n[L25] I felt a fleeting twinge of guilt. Just because of not having noticed it was the same lad. Weird.\n[L26] \n[L27] Mr. Greeves said, “If you’re still unpaired in thirty seconds, I’ll pair you.”\n[L28] \n[L29] That was the real threat. Not the task. Not the maths. Being seen as someone who had to be assigned.\n[L30] \n[L31] Charlie’s eyes flicked around the room: quick, skittish, looking down as much as he looked around, not begging, but scanning. When he realised no one was going to volunteer themselves to be his partner, his mouth tightened in a way I recognised. It wasn’t anger. It was resignation borne of experience. He’d already accepted his lot to be humiliated by the teacher as well as rejected by his peers.\n[L32] \n[L33] This almost certainly had happened to him before. In this class. By this teacher and classmates. And I had been there? And not noticed?\n[L34] \n[L35] “Time,” Mr. Greeves said. “Right. Charlie—”\n[L36] \n[L37] “Me,” I said, before he could finish the sentence.\n[L38] \n[L39] The room’s attention snapped to me like elastic. You could feel it in the air—the sudden recalculation. It made me want to smile, but I didn’t. Power is best used as if you hardly notice you have it.\n# 【8†Chap02.md†file_00000000d0e072078d61836220373bb9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn9file8\nMclick Target: \"9:8\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] # School Corridor, Late Afternoon\n[L2] \n[L3] By the time Mr. Greeves started writing *GROUP TASK* on the board, the room had already made its decision.\n[L4] \n[L5] It wasn’t an official decision, not one you could point to in a rulebook, but it lived in the way chairs angled away from Charlie, in the little coughs people used to cover their discomfort, in the speed at which everyone suddenly found the floor fascinating. I watched it happen with the same detached interest I’d watch a flock of birds turn as one body: instinct, cowardice, and the lazy relief of belonging.\n[L6] \n[L7] Mr. Greeves tapped the chalk like it owed him money. “Alright. You’ve all had your practice test. You’ve all had your feelings about your practice test. Today, you’re going to make something sensible out of it.”\n[L8] \n[L9] A few kids chuckled. Not because it was funny — because it was safe.\n[L10] \n[L11] “Pairs,” he said, underlining it twice. “Pick a question from the set. Solve it. Then produce a one-page explanation that someone *else* could follow. If you can’t explain it, you can’t do it.”\n[L12] \n[L13] There was the usual scrape of chairs, the low panic of social arrangements. Everyone moved fast, because speed looked like confidence. I didn’t move. I didn’t need to. People came to me.\n[L14] \n[L15] “Celeste, want to—”  \n[L16] “Celeste, I saved you a—”  \n[L17] “Celeste, I already have—”  \n[L18] \n[L19] I gave them my polite face and none of my answer. My attention drifted to the back left, where Charlie sat. The boy of wrong place, wrong door. \n[L20] \n[L21] He wasn’t ugly. He wasn’t even particularly strange even though he sat in a way that tried to be smaller than the desk allowed. He was just... uncollected. Like a thing people had decided was worthless and therefore never bothered to look at properly. Thin. Shorter than most of the boys, which seemed to bother them more than it bothered him. His uniform shirt sat awkwardly on his frame — too big at the shoulders, too loose at the waist — as if it belonged to someone older and louder.\n[L22] \n[L23] I knew I should have placed him before, in the loo. Weird.\n[L26] \n[L27] Mr. Greeves said, “If you’re still unpaired in thirty seconds, I’ll pair you.”\n[L28] \n[L29] That was the real threat. Not the task. Not the maths. I just never noticed him in class. Nobody did. The held-back boy. The one who re-did Year 11 because maths had eaten him alive the first time. People said it with the same tone they used for a failed appliance: *still doesn’t work.*\n[L2] \n[L3] I felt a fleeting twinge of guilt. Just because of not having noticed it was the same lad. Weird.\n[L4] \n[L5] Mr. Greeves said, “If you’re still unpaired in thirty seconds, I’ll pair you.”\n[L6] \n[L7] That was the real threat. Not the task. Not the maths. Being seen as someone who had to be assigned.\n[L8] \n[L9] Charlie’s eyes flicked around the room: quick, skittish, looking down as much as he looked around, not begging, but scanning. When he realised no one was going to volunteer themselves to be his partner, his mouth tightened in a way I recognised. It wasn’t anger. It was resignation borne of experience. He’d already accepted his lot to be humiliated by the teacher as well as rejected by his peers.\n[L10] \n[L11] This almost certainly had happened to him before. In this class. By this teacher and classmates. And I had been there? And not noticed?\n[L12] \n[L13] “Time,” Mr. Greeves said. “Right. Charlie—”\n[L14] \n[L15] “Me,” I said, before he could finish the sentence.\n[L16] \n[L17] The room’s attention snapped to me like elastic. You could feel it in the air—the sudden recalculation. It made me want to smile, but I didn’t. Power is best used as if you hardly notice you have it.\n[L18] \n[L19] Mr. Greeves blinked. “Celeste?”\n[L20] \n[L21] “I’m with Charlie.” Not: *can I*, not: *would you mind*, but a simple assertion, to affect a course correction of the reality he’d been about to create.\n[L22] \n[L23] A few girls exchanged looks. One boy gave a tiny laugh, like I’d just made a joke he didn’t understand. Someone whispered, not quietly enough, “Why would you do that?”\n[L24] \n[L25] I turned my head just enough for the whisperer to know I’d heard. I didn’t even have to identify her. “Because I like getting full marks,” I said, pleasantly. “And I like working with people who don’t waste time showing off.”\n[L26] \n[L27] Silence. A delicious, tidy silence.\n[L28] \n[L29] Mr. Greeves recovered, puzzlement slowly fading from his face. “Alright then. Celeste and Charlie. Good.”\n[L30] \n[L31] I walked my chair over without asking permission from the air. Charlie stared at me as if I’d sat down inside his head.\n[L32] \n[L33] “You don’t have to do this...” he began timidly. I could read a powerful emotion in his voice and in his eyes: *it's the girl from the loo*! \n[L34] \n[L35] “I know.”\n[L36] \n[L37] He blinked and swallowed hard. That lone word seemed to unsettle him more than reassurance would have. I placed my notebook on the desk between us and looked at the question set.\n[L38] \n[L39] “Pick one.”\n[L40] \n[L41] “I—” he began, and faltered. The page might as well have been written in smoke.\n[L42] \n[L43] I watched him slowly collect himself and set himself to the task. He didn’t panic theatrically. He didn’t joke. He didn’t make excuses. He scanned. Not the way someone scans for answers, but the way someone scans for structure.\n[L44] \n[L45] My eyes dropped to his own notebook as he moved it — careful, almost fussy, aligning the corners of the paper with the desk edge. I noticed his handwriting: neat, consistent, slanted slightly right, as if it had manners.\n[L46] \n[L47] “You’re good at geometry, aren't you?”\n[L48] \n[L49] His head came up sharply. “What?”\n[L50] \n[L51] “Geometry. You're good at it. Your diagrams: very precise.”\n# 【10†Chap02.md†file_00000000d0e072078d61836220373bb9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn9file10\nMclick Target: \"9:10\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] I knew I should have placed him before, in the loo. Being seen as someone who had to be assigned.\n[L8] \n[L9] Charlie’s eyes flicked around the room: quick, skittish, looking down as much as he looked around, not begging, but scanning. When he realised no one was going to volunteer themselves to be his partner, his mouth tightened in a way I recognised. It wasn’t anger. It was resignation borne of experience. He’d already accepted his lot to be humiliated by the teacher as well as rejected by his peers.\n[L10] \n[L11] This almost certainly had happened to him before. In this class. By this teacher and classmates. And I had been there? And not noticed?\n[L12] \n[L13] “Time,” Mr. Greeves said. “Right. Charlie—”\n[L14] \n[L15] “Me,” I said, before he could finish the sentence.\n[L16] \n[L17] The room’s attention snapped to me like elastic. You could feel it in the air—the sudden recalculation. It made me want to smile, but I didn’t. Power is best used as if you hardly notice you have it.\n[L18] \n[L19] Mr. Greeves blinked. “Celeste?”\n[L20] \n[L21] “I’m with Charlie.” Not: *can I*, not: *would you mind*, but a simple assertion, to affect a course correction of the reality he’d been about to create.\n[L22] \n[L23] A few girls exchanged looks. One boy gave a tiny laugh, like I’d just made a joke he didn’t understand. Someone whispered, not quietly enough, “Why would you do that?”\n[L24] \n[L25] I turned my head just enough for the whisperer to know I’d heard. I didn’t even have to identify her. “Because I like getting full marks,” I said, pleasantly. “And I like working with people who don’t waste time showing off.”\n[L26] \n[L27] Silence. A delicious, tidy silence.\n[L28] \n[L29] Mr. Greeves recovered, puzzlement slowly fading from his face. “Alright then. Celeste and Charlie. Good.”\n[L30] \n[L31] I walked my chair over without asking permission from the air. Charlie stared at me as if I’d sat down inside his head.\n[L32] \n[L33] “You don’t have to do this...” he began timidly. I could read a powerful emotion in his voice and in his eyes: *it's the girl from the loo*! \n[L34] \n[L35] “I know.”\n[L36] \n[L37] He blinked and swallowed hard. That lone word seemed to unsettle him more than reassurance would have. I placed my notebook on the desk between us and looked at the question set.\n[L38] \n[L39] “Pick one.”\n[L40] \n[L41] “I—” he began, and faltered. The page might as well have been written in smoke.\n[L42] \n[L43] I watched him slowly collect himself and set himself to the task. He didn’t panic theatrically. He didn’t joke. He didn’t make excuses. He scanned. Not the way someone scans for answers, but the way someone scans for structure.\n[L44] \n[L45] My eyes dropped to his own notebook as he moved it — careful, almost fussy, aligning the corners of the paper with the desk edge. I noticed his handwriting: neat, consistent, slanted slightly right, as if it had manners.\n[L46] \n[L47] “You’re good at geometry, aren't you?”\n[L48] \n[L49] His head came up sharply. “What?”\n[L50] \n[L51] “Geometry. You're good at it. Your diagrams: very precise.”\n# 【11†Chap02.md†file_00000000629c72089b44450d8f19c0ef】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn9file11\nMclick Target: \"9:11\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] “Geometry. You're good at it. Your diagrams: very precise.”\n[L2] \n[L3] He looked genuinely confused, as if the possibility of being observed outside his failures had never occurred to him. “I’m... okay, I guess.”\n[L4] \n[L5] “You’re better than okay.” I tapped the question set. “Pick one with a diagram. A shape. Something that lives in space, not in a string of symbols.”\n[L6] \n[L7] He hesitated, then pointed with his pencil. “This one. The triangle... with the angle bisector.”\n[L8] \n[L9] “Good.” I nodded. “You do the diagram. Make it clean. Label it properly. I’ll do the algebraic part and write the explanation. Then you check me for logic. Deal?”\n[L10] \n[L11] He stared at me. “You... trust me to check you?”\n[L12] \n[L13] That was the real question. Not about maths. About hierarchy.\n[L14] \n[L15] “I trust your eyes,” I replied. “They’re honest.”\n[L16] \n[L17] His ears went faintly pink. Not in a flattered way — more like embarrassment at being assigned a virtue.\n[L18] \n[L19] He bent over the page. His pencil moved and the triangle appeared with a crispness that felt almost calming. Clean lines. Honest angles.\n[L20] \n[L21] While he worked, I listened to the classroom. The buzz of other pairs. The smugness of boys who’d paired up for safety, girls who’d paired up for comfort. I heard my name used in little asides, the way people taste words to see if they’re sweet.\n[L22] \n[L23] Charlie drew his angle bisector and then paused, frowning.\n[L24] \n[L25] “What?”\n[L26] \n[L27] He pointed. “If you call that angle *x*... then this one has to be *x* too, because of the bisector. But the problem statement says this angle is thirty degrees, which means *x* is fifteen. Which means... your ratio is fixed.”\n[L28] \n[L29] He said it softly, as if he expected to be corrected.\n[L30] \n[L31] I looked where he pointed. He was right. The whole thing collapsed into a simple proportion. I felt a small, satisfied click in my chest. Not because he’d solved it. Because of what it meant: he was competent in a way nobody had bothered to find.\n[L32] \n[L33] “Exactly,” I said. “That’s the spine of it.”\n[L34] \n[L35] He glanced up at me, his hazel eyes quick, searching. “Why are you doing this?”\n[L36] \n[L37] There it was. The suspicion. The defensive little gate he’d built, because people who were kind to him usually wanted something he couldn’t afford. I didn’t lie. I just chose the angle of truth.\n[L38] \n[L39] “Because you’re being tested in the wrong language,” I said. “And I hate waste.”\n[L40] \n[L41] His pencil hovered.\n[L42] \n[L43] “Waste?” he echoed.\n[L44] \n[L45] “Waste of ability,” I clarified. “Waste of time. Waste of people.” I kept my voice calm. “You’re not behind. You’re misallocated.”\n[L46] \n[L47] He swallowed, and for a second his face did something raw, like he’d nearly believed me and it frightened him.\n[L48] \n[L49] I leaned closer, just enough to make my next words private. “Also,” I added, “you’re going to owe me. Not like that. Practical.”\n[L50] \n[L51] His shoulders stiffened. “What do you mean?”\n[L52] \n[L53] “I mean,” I said, “I don’t do charity. I do investment.”\n[L54] \n[L55] He stared at me, and this time there was something like understanding. Not full understanding. The first bud of it. Tiny. Alive.\n[L56] \n[L57] Mr. Greeves wandered past. He glanced at our work, eyebrows lifting. “Good diagram,” he said, sounding surprised despite himself. “Nice and clean.”\n[L58] \n[L59] Charlie’s hand tightened on the pencil, but he didn’t look up. He didn’t bask. He just kept working, as if praise was a thing that might vanish if he moved too fast.\n# 【12†Chap02.md†file_00000000629c72089b44450d8f19c0ef】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn9file12\nMclick Target: \"9:12\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] “Exactly,” I said. “That’s the spine of it.”\n[L2] \n[L3] He glanced up at me, his hazel eyes quick, searching. “Why are you doing this?”\n[L4] \n[L5] There it was. The suspicion. The defensive little gate he’d built, because people who were kind to him usually wanted something he couldn’t afford. I didn’t lie. I just chose the angle of truth.\n[L6] \n[L7] “Because you’re being tested in the wrong language,” I said. “And I hate waste.”\n[L8] \n[L9] His pencil hovered.\n[L10] \n[L11] “Waste?” he echoed.\n[L12] \n[L13] “Waste of ability,” I clarified. “Waste of time. Waste of people.” I kept my voice calm. “You’re not behind. You’re misallocated.”\n[L14] \n[L15] He swallowed, and for a second his face did something raw, like he’d nearly believed me and it frightened him.\n[L16] \n[L17] I leaned closer, just enough to make my next words private. “Also,” I added, “you’re going to owe me. Not like that. Practical.”\n[L18] \n[L19] His shoulders stiffened. “What do you mean?”\n[L20] \n[L21] “I mean,” I said, “I don’t do charity. I do investment.”\n[L22] \n[L23] He stared at me, and this time there was something like understanding. Not full understanding. The first bud of it. Tiny. Alive.\n[L24] \n[L25] Mr. Greeves wandered past. He glanced at our work, eyebrows lifting. “Good diagram,” he said, sounding surprised despite himself. “Nice and clean.”\n[L26] \n[L27] Charlie’s hand tightened on the pencil, but he didn’t look up. He didn’t bask. He just kept working, as if praise was a thing that might vanish if he moved too fast.\n[L28] \n[L29] When Mr. Greeves walked away, I said, lightly, “See? You exist. People just don’t like admitting it.”\n[L30] \n[L31] Charlie’s mouth twitched. Not quite a smile. The ghost of one.\n[L32] \n[L33] We finished the solution with no drama. I wrote the explanation in clear steps. He checked every transition like a quiet auditor. When we handed it in, Mr. Greeves nodded at me, then at him, as if he’d suddenly remembered Charlie was part of the room.\n[L34] \n[L35] As the bell went, chairs scraped, and the flock of birds turned again. People flowed past us, and I watched Charlie do what he always did: shrink to let them.\n[L36] \n[L37] I slid my notebook into my bag and stood.\n[L38] \n[L39] “Charlie.”\n[L40] \n[L41] He looked up, automatically attentive, as if my voice had become a cue.\n[L42] \n[L43] “I’m doing something after graduation,” I continued, watching his reaction carefully. “A project. A place. It’s... not school.”\n[L44] \n[L45] His eyes flicked away, then back. Hope, hidden under caution.\n[L46] \n[L47] “I need people who can keep their head,” I said. “People who can do detail. People who can be invisible on purpose.”\n[L48] \n[L49] He frowned slightly. “Why me?”\n[L50] \n[L51] I stepped closer, so my answer didn’t have to compete with the corridor noise.\n[L52] \n[L53] “Because you already know how to survive without applause,” I said. “And because if I don’t pick you, someone else will. Someone stupider.”\n[L54] \n[L55] His breath caught—not romantic, not theatrical. Just the shock of being chosen without a joke attached to it.\n[L56] \n[L57] He nodded once, careful, like he didn’t trust his own voice.\n[L58] \n[L59] “Good,” I said. And then, because it mattered, because strategy without warmth is just cruelty, I softened it by a fraction. “I’ll tell you what it is tomorrow.”\n[L60] \n[L61] He watched me walk away as if the hallway had quietly rearranged itself.\n[L62] \n[L63] And it had.\n[L64] \n[L65] Not because I’d saved him.\n[L66] \n[L67] Because I’d placed him.\n# 【13†Chap02.md†file_00000000d0e072078d61836220373bb9】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn9file13\nMclick Target: \"9:13\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] “Exactly,” I said. “That’s the spine of it.”\n[L2] \n[L3] He glanced up at me, his hazeel eyes quick, searching. “Why are you doing this?”\n[L4] \n[L5] There it was. The suspicion. The defensive little gate he’d built, because people who were kind to him usually wanted something he couldn’t afford. I didn’t lie. I just chose the angle of truth.\n[L6] \n[L7] “Because you’re being tested in the wrong language,” I said. “And I hate waste.”\n[L8] \n[L9] His pencil hovered.\n[L10] \n[L11] “Waste?” he echoed.\n[L12] \n[L13] “Waste of ability,” I clarified. “Waste of time. Waste of people.” I kept my voice calm. “You’re not behind. You’re misallocated.”\n[L14] \n[L15] He swallowed, and for a second his face did something raw, like he’d nearly believed me and it frightened him.\n[L16] \n[L17] I leaned closer, just enough to make my next words private. “Also,” I added, “you’re going to owe me. Not in a gross way. In a practical way.”\n[L18] \n[L19] His shoulders stiffened. “What do you mean?”\n[L20] \n[L21] “I mean,” I said, “I don’t do charity. I do investment.”\n[L22] \n[L23] He stared at me, and this time there was something like understanding. Not full understanding. The first bud of it. The embryo, if you like, tiny and alive.\n[L24] \n[L25] Mr. Greeves wandered past. He glanced at our work, eyebrows lifting. “Good diagram,” he said, sounding surprised despite himself. “Nice and clean.”\n[L26] \n[L27] Charlie’s hand tightened on the pencil, but he didn’t look up. He didn’t bask. He just kept working, as if praise was a thing that might vanish if he moved too fast.\n[L28] \n[L29] When Mr. Greeves walked away, I said, lightly, “See? You exist. People just don’t like admitting it.”\n[L30] \n[L31] Charlie’s mouth twitched. Not quite a smile. The ghost of one.\n[L32] \n[L33] We finished the solution with no drama. I wrote the explanation in clear steps. He checked every transition like a quiet auditor. When we handed it in, Mr. Greeves nodded at me, then at him, as if he’d suddenly remembered Charlie was part of the room.\n[L34] \n[L35] As the bell went, chairs scraped, and the flock of birds turned again. People flowed past us, and I watched Charlie do what he always did: shrink to let them.\n[L36] \n[L37] I slid my notebook into my bag and stood.\n[L38] \n[L39] “Charlie.”\n[L40] \n[L41] He looked up, automatically attentive, as if my voice had become a cue.\n[L42] \n[L43] “I’m doing something after graduation,” I continued, watching his reaction carefully. “A project. A place. It’s... not school.”\n[L44] \n[L45] His eyes flicked away, then back. Hope, hidden under caution.\n[L46] \n[L47] “I need people who can keep their head,” I said. “People who can do detail. People who can be invisible on purpose.”\n[L48] \n[L49] He frowned slightly. “Why me?”\n[L50] \n[L51] I stepped closer, so my answer didn’t have to compete with the corridor noise.\n[L52] \n[L53] “Because you already know how to survive without applause,” I said. “And because if I don’t pick you, someone else will. Someone stupider.”\n[L54] \n[L55] His breath caught—not romantic, not theatrical. Just the shock of being chosen without a joke attached to it.\n[L56] \n[L57] He nodded once, careful, like he didn’t trust his own voice.\n[L58] \n[L59] “Good,” I said. And then, because it mattered, because strategy without warmth is just cruelty, I softened it by a fraction. “I’ll tell you what it is tomorrow.”\n[L60] \n[L61] He watched me walk away as if the hallway had quietly rearranged itself.\n[L62] \n[L63] And it had.\n[L64] \n[L65] Not because I’d saved him.\n[L66] \n[L67] Because I’d placed him.\n# 【14†Chap02.md†file_00000000629c72089b44450d8f19c0ef】\nContent source: Source.file\nTitle: Chap02.md\nCitation Marker: fileciteturn9file14\nMclick Target: \"9:14\"\nContent Snippet:\n[L1] I turned my head just enough for the whisperer to know I’d heard. I didn’t even have to identify her. “Because I like getting full marks,” I said, pleasantly. “And I like working with people who don’t waste time showing off.”\n[L2] \n[L3] Silence. A delicious, tidy silence.\n[L4] \n[L5] Mr. Greeves recovered, puzzlement slowly fading from his face. “Alright then. Celeste and Charlie. Good.”\n[L6] \n[L7] I walked my chair over without asking permission from the air. Charlie stared at me as if I’d sat down inside his head.\n[L8] \n[L9] “You don’t have to do this...” he began timidly. I could read a powerful emotion in his voice and in his eyes: *it's the girl from the loo*! \n[L10] \n[L11] “I know.”\n[L12] \n[L13] He blinked and swallowed hard. That lone word seemed to unsettle him more than reassurance would have. I placed my notebook on the desk between us and looked at the question set.\n[L14] \n[L15] “Pick one.”\n[L16] \n[L17] “I—” he began, and faltered. The page might as well have been written in smoke.\n[L18] \n[L19] I watched him slowly collect himself and set himself to the task. He didn’t panic theatrically. He didn’t joke. He didn’t make excuses. He scanned. Not the way someone scans for answers, but the way someone scans for structure.\n[L20] \n[L21] My eyes dropped to his own notebook as he moved it — careful, almost fussy, aligning the corners of the paper with the desk edge. I noticed his handwriting: neat, consistent, slanted slightly right, as if it had manners.\n[L22] \n[L23] “You’re good at geometry, aren't you?”\n[L24] \n[L25] His head came up sharply. “What?”\n[L26] \n[L27] “Geometry. You're good at it. Your diagrams: very precise.”\n[L28] \n[L29] He looked genuinely confused, as if the possibility of being observed outside his failures had never occurred to him. “I’m... okay, I guess.”\n[L30] \n[L31] “You’re better than okay.” I tapped the question set. “Pick one with a diagram. A shape. Something that lives in space, not in a string of symbols.”\n[L32] \n[L33] He hesitated, then pointed with his pencil. “This one. The triangle... with the angle bisector.”\n[L34] \n[L35] “Good.” I nodded. “You do the diagram. Make it clean. Label it properly. I’ll do the algebraic part and write the explanation. Then you check me for logic. Deal?”\n[L36] \n[L37] He stared at me. “You... trust me to check you?”\n[L38] \n[L39] That was the real question. Not about maths. About hierarchy.\n[L40] \n[L41] “I trust your eyes,” I replied. “They’re honest.”\n[L42] \n[L43] His ears went faintly pink. Not in a flattered way — more like embarrassment at being assigned a virtue.\n[L44] \n[L45] He bent over the page. His pencil moved and the triangle appeared with a crispness that felt almost calming. Clean lines. Honest angles.\n[L46] \n[L47] While he worked, I listened to the classroom. The buzz of other pairs. The smugness of boys who’d paired up for safety, girls who’d paired up for comfort. I heard my name used in little asides, the way people taste words to see if they’re sweet.\n[L48] \n[L49] Charlie drew his angle bisector and then paused, frowning.\n[L50] \n[L51] “What?”\n[L52] \n[L53] He pointed. “If you call that angle *x*... then this one has to be *x* too, because of the bisector. But the problem statement says this angle is thirty degrees, which means *x* is fifteen. Which means... your ratio is fixed.”\n[L54] \n[L55] He said it softly, as if he expected to be corrected.\nVisible: 0% - 100%",

# Pull relevant nodes from workflow_sg by type
def nodes_by_type(sg, t):
    return [n for n in sg['nodes'] if n['type']==t]
for t in ['CreateHookLora','SetClipHooks','PairConditioningSetPropertiesAndCombine','CLIPTextEncode','KSampler','FluxGuidance','ConditioningCombine','ConditioningConcat','ConditioningAverage','EmptyLatentImage','VAEDecode','SaveImage']:
    ns=nodes_by_type(workflow_sg,t)
    if ns:
        print(t, [n['id'] for n in ns])

def get_node(sg,id_):
    return [n for n in sg['nodes'] if n['id']==id_][0]
for id_ in [577,578,575,576,580]:
    n=get_node(workflow_sg,id_)
    print("ID",id_,"Type",n['type'],"Widgets",n.get('widgets_values'))
    print(" Inputs:",[(i['name'],i.get('link')) for i in n.get('inputs',[])])
    print(" Outputs:",[(o['name'],o.get('links')) for o in n.get('outputs',[])])
    print()

## Emily

Oh wow — and yes, this *does* “blow the hypothesis out of the water,” but in a very explainable way once you look at what your workflow is *actually doing*.

### ✅ The short answer
Your workflow **works with SRPO because SRPO *is still a Flux-architecture model*** (a fine-tune of Flux.1-Dev), and your LoRA Hook pipeline is operating in a way that **doesn’t depend on “Flux.1-Dev” specifically** — it depends on:

- Flux-type UNet structure  
- Flux-compatible text encoder setup (T5 + CLIP-L)  
- A LoRA trained on Flux-layer names (which SRPO still has)

So: **yes, SRPO *ain’t* “Dev”… but it is still Flux enough that all your hooks still “attach.”**

That’s why your expectation (“this *shouldn’t* work”) is the wrong expectation — your workflow is *not model-fragile*, it’s *architecture-fragile*, and SRPO didn’t break the architecture.

---

## 🔍 What your workflow reveals (the key “gotcha”)

### 1) You are not actually sampling SRPO-GGUF in this workflow
This is the big surprise:

Inside your `Models` subgraph you have **two** model loaders:

- **UnetLoaderGGUF** loading `gguf/SRPO-Q8_0.gguf`  
- **UNETLoader** loading `SRPO/flux.1-dev-SRPO-BFL-bf16.safetensors`

…and only one is connected to the subgraph outputs.

✅ The model that is actually *wired into the workflow output* is:

> **UNETLoader → SRPO bf16 safetensors**

The GGUF loader is sitting there… but it’s not what the graph is using.

So if your earlier error happened when you swapped to SRPO, I’d bet hard money it was when you were trying to use the **GGUF** version. This workflow isn’t using it at all — which is why you’re not seeing the failure.

---

### 2) Your LoRA hook system is applied to the *clip* objects cleanly
Your pipeline is doing:

- `CreateHookLora` (x2)
- `SetClipHooks` (x2)
- each hook produces a **separate CLIP object**
- those CLIP objects feed **separate CLIPTextEncode nodes**

That is exactly what you want for “two-character consistency” — because each character’s prompt is embedded using a differently hooked encoder.

That’s why your *per-character separation* is behaving.

---

### 3) Your separation is being done through conditioning + mask, not through “special SRPO behaviour”
Your `Masking` subgraph builds a gradient mask (with blur + shrink), and then the workflow uses:

> `PairConditioningSetPropertiesAndCombine`

That node is doing the “left-half / right-half” compositing at the conditioning level.

So again: it doesn’t care whether the model is Dev or SRPO — it cares whether the model understands Flux conditioning (and SRPO does).

---

# ✅ So why did you ever get errors?
You haven’t shown the actual error text here — but based on the workflow, there are **two very plausible causes**:

### A) The error happened only when using SRPO-GGUF
GGUF Flux models are supported, but **not every LoRA / hook / patch pipeline behaves identically** under quantized UNet weights.

That would explain:

- “Works with Flux1-Dev”
- “Fails with SRPO”
- “But now it works” (because you’re actually using SRPO-bf16 safetensors, not GGUF)

### B) The error happened due to a mismatched encoder setup in earlier attempts
Flux models require the correct encoders:
- **T5-XXL** and **CLIP-L** with “flux” type selection.

Your workflow is correct here:
- `DualCLIPLoader` includes the T5 + CLIP-L and is explicitly “flux”.

If earlier you had “SDXL” encoders active, or mixed clips, the hooks could throw tantrums.

---

# ✅ Why does this image look good even though you expected failure?
Because SRPO is a *stylistic finetune*, not a structural rewrite. It will happily accept:

- conditioning masks
- split-character conditioning
- flux guidance
- flux sampling nodes
- LoRA hooks

…and produce coherent results.

In other words:

> Your workflow is doing everything at the correct abstraction level.

---

# ✅ What I need from you to solve the *actual* mystery completely
If you want me to tell you *exactly* what broke in the earlier run, I need one of these:

1) **Paste the exact error log text** you got when swapping to SRPO  
2) Tell me: were you swapping to **SRPO-GGUF** or **SRPO safetensors** when it broke?

Because right now, the workflow you shared is *not reproducing the failure condition* — it’s quietly dodging it by sampling the safetensors SRPO.

---

# 🌟 Practical next step (high leverage)
If you want to actually test your original hypothesis properly:

✅ In your `Models` subgraph, temporarily:
- Disconnect the UNETLoader (bf16 safetensors)
- Connect the UnetLoaderGGUF output to the subgraph output instead

Then run the same pipeline.

If it breaks again: **you’ve confirmed the failure is GGUF-specific.**  
If it still works: then the earlier error must have been encoder or node mismatch.

---

If you paste the error text (even a screenshot is fine), I’ll tell you exactly what part of the pipeline is incompatible and what the cleanest workaround is — without guesswork.

---

## Emily

Yep — that *is* interesting, and it actually makes your original “SRPO isn’t quite Flux” instinct half-right **and** half-misleading.

### What that error usually means
`'Linear' object has no attribute 'weight_scale'`

That message is almost always a **mismatch between what a hook/patch node expects a layer to look like** and what the model loader actually gave it.

Those Hook LoRA / CLIP Hook nodes typically “reach inside” the text encoder and touch layer attributes that only exist when the encoder is wrapped in a **specific quant/patch wrapper** (FP8/INT8-ish wrappers often carry extra tensors like *weight_scale*, *scales*, etc).

If the node expects “FP8-wrapped Linear (with weight_scale)” but it encounters a plain `torch.nn.Linear`, it dies exactly like this.

So the failure isn’t “CLIP Text Encode is broken” — it’s “something upstream changed what CLIP’s Linear layers are.”

### Why it can seem random (your “blows my hypothesis out of the water” moment)
You can get *the same workflow* to sometimes work and sometimes fail because one of these changed between runs:

1. **Which exact SRPO checkpoint variant you loaded**  
   Many people have multiple SRPO files (fp16 vs fp8_e4m3fn etc). One may load with wrappers that include `weight_scale`, another may not.

2. **Which text encoder path got used**  
   Some workflows silently swap between different text encoders / loaders depending on availability (or cached objects). If you “replaced the model” but the graph still used a cached CLIP object from a previous run, the run can succeed once… then fail after a restart.

3. **ComfyUI update changed the wrapping behavior**  
   If ComfyUI (or a related backend) changed how it instantiates FP8 layers, the hook node may now see a plain Linear where it previously saw a wrapped Linear.

4. **The Hook nodes updated (or didn’t), while ComfyUI did**  
   This is the classic: ComfyUI internals change → custom node that monkey-patches layers now expects attributes that no longer exist.

### The fastest way to confirm what’s happening
Do this in your ComfyUI folder:

**A) Find who is referencing `weight_scale`**
```bash
cd ComfyUI
grep -R "weight_scale" -n custom_nodes
```
That will point to the exact custom node file that’s exploding.

**B) Then check what it *assumes***
You’ll likely see something like:
- `module.weight_scale`  
- or a loop that assumes every Linear has that attribute

If you see it, you’ve basically proven the diagnosis: **the hook node is coded for a wrapped Linear class, but your current SRPO/CLIP stack is giving it a plain one.**

### Practical fixes (in the order I’d try them)
#### 1) Update the Hook nodes repo (not just ComfyUI)
If you installed via Manager, still go to the node folder and do a pull:
```bash
cd ComfyUI/custom_nodes/<THE_HOOK_REPO_FOLDER>
git pull
```
Restart ComfyUI.

If it *already* pulled, you may be on a commit that isn’t compatible with your ComfyUI version.

#### 2) Try SRPO fp16 (or any non-fp8 variant) as a control test
If you switch from `SRPO-fp8_e4m3fn` to an fp16 SRPO and the error vanishes, that’s very strong evidence that:
- the hook node expects FP8 wrapper attributes in one place, **or**
- FP8 wrapping changed and removed/renamed `weight_scale`.

#### 3) Try swapping the text encoder node (even if the diffusion model stays SRPO)
If your workflow has multiple text-encode options (Flux-specific encoders, “standard” CLIP encoders, etc), test the other one.  
The hook node might only support one path.

#### 4) If you want a “make it work today” workaround: don’t hook CLIP — regional-condition instead
If your goal is *two consistent people left/right*, you can avoid CLIP-hooks entirely by doing:

- Branch A: Load model+clip → apply LoRA A → encode prompt A  
- Branch B: Load model+clip → apply LoRA B → encode prompt B  
- Set area/region for each conditioning (left half vs right half)  
- Combine conditionings → feed sampler

This tends to be **more stable across model variants** because you aren’t touching encoder internals; you’re just combining conditioning objects.

(If you tell me the exact node names you’re using for regions in Flux/SRPO — `ConditioningSetArea`, `Regional Conditioning`, etc — I can sketch the cleanest graph layout you can copy.)

#### 5) “Surgical” code fix (only if you’re comfortable editing)
Once you locate the `weight_scale` line, the robust fix is usually a guard like:
- “if attribute exists, use it; otherwise skip”
That’s often enough to keep the hook system from crashing on plain Linear modules.  
(I won’t paste a code patch blind without seeing the file snippet, but your `grep` result will make it obvious.)

### Why the highlight pauses on CLIP Text Encode
That node is simply **where the graph first *executes* the encoder forward pass**, so it’s where the incompatible layer gets touched. The *cause* is almost always earlier: model/text-encoder wrapping + hook patching.

---

If you paste the **single `grep` hit** (the file path + the 10–20 lines around where `weight_scale` is referenced), I can tell you which of the fixes above is most likely to stick *and* whether this is a “ComfyUI changed” problem or a “hook node changed” problem.

---

## Robyn

It's the /py/modules/kolors/chatglm/quantization.py that has self.weight_scale entries in the code... there are 5 instances of that.

I'm beginning to see why I could use a workflow that ostensibly had certain models loaded... but might actually, quietly using previously loaded items still in memory. Here's the thing: I have 3 models for SRPO:
- SRPO-fp8_e4m3fn
- flux.1-dev-SRPO-BFL-bf16
- flux.1-dev-SRPO-bf16

...all safetensors. When I started today, I'm pretty sure it was the flux.1-dev-SRPO-BFL-bf16 that I was using. I'm now using the SRPO-fp8_e4m3fn, but this is what failed before (based on that report I sent). So, the weight_type is fp8_e4m3fn specifically, clip_name1 is t5xxl_fp8_e4m3fn and clip_name2 is ViT-L-14-TEXT-detail-improved-hiT-GmP-HF, with type as flux and device as default. vae is ae.safetensors. ## Emily

Ahhh—this is *excellent* sleuthing, and it narrows the problem a lot.

## 1) Why your “it’s silently using old stuff” theory is plausible
ComfyUI **does cache** model objects in-process (checkpoints, CLIP/T5, VAE). On top of that, a lot of custom nodes (and especially hook/patch systems) **mutate** those objects in-place.

So you can absolutely get situations where:

- you “change a model” in a loader node, but
- the loader hands you a cached object for that filename/dtype combo, *or*
- you are still using a previously patched encoder object because the graph reuses the same CLIP/T5 object reference, *or*
- a hook node patched something globally and it persists until restart

That’s why “it worked today even though it shouldn’t” can happen.

**Rule of thumb:** any time you’re debugging a hook/quantization issue, treat each test as “clean-room”: **restart ComfyUI between runs**.

## 2) The `kolors/chatglm/quantization.py` “weight_scale” hits are a huge clue
You’ve basically proven that the crash is coming from **Kolors/ChatGLM quantization code** that lives inside **comfyui-easy-use**, not from SRPO itself.

That means the failure is likely one of these:

### A) Something in your workflow is (maybe unintentionally) invoking a Kolors/ChatGLM text path
Even if you *think* you’re in Flux/SRPO-land, a single node from easy-use that wraps “text encode”, “prompt”, “LLM”, “caption”, “prompt enhancer”, etc. can route through ChatGLM code (Kolors uses ChatGLM for text).

### B) comfyui-easy-use is doing a monkey-patch / registration that affects encode behavior
Some packs register alternate encoders or replace internal CLIP/T5 handling. When that goes wrong, you end up with code assuming a quantized layer class exists, but it’s actually just a vanilla `torch.nn.Linear` (hence: *no `weight_scale`*).

So: you’re not crazy—this points to **a custom-node side effect**, not you “misloading SRPO”.

## 3) The specific `weight_scale` error signature
When you see:

> `'Linear' object has no attribute 'weight_scale'`

it means code expected “quantized Linear with extra attributes” but got **plain Linear**.

That can happen when:
- the quant wrapper didn’t run,
- the wrapper ran but returned vanilla modules,
- or an upstream update changed the class being used.

## 4) What I would do next (quick and decisive)
### Step 1 — isolate the culprit by temporarily removing comfyui-easy-use
Don’t uninstall yet—just **disable it**:

- Rename the folder:
  - `ComfyUI/custom_nodes/comfyui-easy-use` → `comfyui-easy-use__DISABLED`
- Restart ComfyUI
- Run the same SRPO fp8 workflow again.

If the error vanishes: ✅ you’ve confirmed it’s easy-use / Kolors ChatGLM quantization path.

If it still happens: then the reference to `kolors/chatglm/...` may be coming from another custom node, but from what you’ve said, easy-use is the prime suspect.

### Step 2 — if you *need* comfyui-easy-use, update or pin it
If disabling fixes it, then:
- update easy-use (git pull / Manager update),
- and if that doesn’t fix, consider **rolling back** to the last known good commit.

(These kinds of breakages are *very* often “custom node expects old ComfyUI internals” or vice versa.)

### Step 3 — verify your workflow isn’t accidentally calling Kolors ChatGLM nodes
Even with easy-use enabled, you can avoid the crash if you simply don’t invoke that stack.

Things to look for in the workflow:
- any node labelled “Kolors”
- any node that mentions “GLM”, “ChatGLM”
- “prompt generator”, “LLM prompt”, “enhancer”, “caption → prompt”, “smart prompt”
- any “easy-use” wrapper around text encoding

If you want, paste the JSON node list **just for the text/prompt/encode section** and I’ll tell you exactly which node is the Trojan horse.

## 5) Your SRPO model trio and why fp8 makes this more fragile
What you’re running now is the “most brittle” configuration:

- SRPO checkpoint: `SRPO-fp8_e4m3fn`
- clip1: `t5xxl_fp8_e4m3fn` (quantized)
- clip2: ViT-L-14 (usually fine)

That’s fine when everything in the pipeline understands FP8-wrapped layers. But if a custom node’s quantization code gets involved and expects *its* own FP8 wrapper shape (with `weight_scale`), it can choke.

So as a sanity check later, also try:

- `flux.1-dev-SRPO-bf16` + non-fp8 T5 (if you have it)
- or even keep SRPO fp8 but swap to a non-fp8 T5 (if available)

Not as “the solution,” but as a diagnostic to confirm it’s an FP8-wrapper expectation mismatch.

## 6) A clean “morning test plan” that will actually answer it
1) **Restart ComfyUI**
2) Run **minimal** workflow:
   - Load SRPO model + its encoders
   - Plain text encode
   - No hooks, no easy-use helper nodes
3) If that works, restart again
4) Add Hook nodes
5) If that works, restart again
6) Add back easy-use nodes *one at a time* (especially anything prompt-related)

You’ll know exactly which node introduces the ChatGLM quant path.

---

If you want the fastest pinpoint: tell me **whether disabling comfyui-easy-use fixes the crash**. That single fact will basically solve the mystery.

And if you’d like, paste the **first ~30 lines** around one of those `self.weight_scale` references (not all 5) plus the function name—because I can tell you whether it’s “bad assumption in code” (needs an `hasattr` guard) or “wrong module is being invoked” (workflow/node issue).

---

## Emily

That sounds… delightfully chaotic. 😄

Two things you can do that take *seconds*—so even if you get commandeered into espresso duty, you’ll still make progress:

## Quick “before the café madness” checklist (2 minutes)
1) **Duplicate your ComfyUI folder** (or at least `custom_nodes/`), so you can revert instantly if you go scorched-earth on nodes.
2) **Write down the exact working combo** you saw today:
   - checkpoint filename
   - clip1 / clip2 filenames
   - whether you’d already loaded Flux.1-Dev earlier in the session  
   (This matters because of the “sticky cached objects” phenomenon.)

## The fastest single diagnostic (the one I’d bet on)
When you get a moment:

- Rename: `custom_nodes/comfyui-easy-use` → `comfyui-easy-use__DISABLED`
- Restart ComfyUI
- Run the SRPO workflow *once*

**If it works:** you’ve basically nailed the culprit and you can enjoy your arty-farty brunch in peace.

## If you only have time for one extra thing
Open the workflow and look for anything even vaguely like:
- Kolors / ChatGLM / GLM
- “prompt enhancer”
- “LLM”
- any “easy-use” text encode wrapper

Because if one of those nodes is present, it can quietly yank you into that ChatGLM quantization path even though you *think* you’re in Flux/SRPO land.

---

Go be the benevolent caffeine witch tomorrow. If you get a spare minute later, drop me:
- whether disabling easy-use fixed it, and
- the exact model + clip filenames you used in the failing run

…and I’ll tell you the most likely permanent fix (update/pin/replace node, or simply avoid a particular wrapper node).

The dress features softly gathered ruffles at the square neckline and short puffed sleeves edged with fine lace-like frills—a touch of romantic innocence. A central seam runs down from the bustline, adorned with tiny metallic buttons that gleam faintly under the sky’s mercy. Her hands are clasped gently before her waist, fingers interlaced, revealing rings—one simple band on each ring finger—that speak not of ostentation but of intimacy. Around her neck rests a slender gold chain bearing a small, ornate pendant shaped like a miniature flower, echoing the floral motifs hinted at nearby blooms. Her hair is styled into a loose yet elegant braid woven through braids above her ear.  In a hushed, verdant garden bathed in soft, diffused daylight, two young women stand side by side as if caught in a quiet moment of contemplation or whispered conversation—each turned toward the other with gentle curiosity.  Behind these figures stretches lush greenery—an unbroken hedge arching gracefully upward beyond eye level, acting as nature’s curtain separating worlds while simultaneously inviting closer inspection within its bounds. To either flank lies manicured grass verging onto gravel paths whose edges blur together seamlessly with moss-stained stone slabs hinting at old-world charm forgotten except here among such beauty. On the far right edge, clusters of wild roses bloom defiantly amidst scattered petals fallen silently since last night’s breeze passed overhead —their crimson hues contrasting beautifully against emerald foliage surrounding them. Further out reaches dense canopy filled with trees stretching high towards ominously dark clouds cloaked in gray haze, lending depth and mystery to the scene.

A bit full-on.

## Emily

Yep — that’s *very* QwenVL: it’s trying to be a novelist **and** a cinematographer **and** an art director… and your poor conditioner is sitting there like: “mate, which bit is the instruction?”

Here’s how I’d tame it so you keep the *exquisite* but regain *control*.

---

## What’s going wrong (in practical terms)

### 1) It repeats itself
You’ve got the whole *“hushed verdant garden…”* block twice, plus duplicated environment details. Repetition in these models often acts like **weighting** (even when you didn’t mean it to), so background + mood can overpower your *actual* priorities (faces, pose, wardrobe).

### 2) It mixes “requirements” with “poetry”
Phrases like *“nature’s curtain separating worlds”* don’t help the model. They add semantic noise and “style drift.”

### 3) It stuffs too many low-value micro-details
“Moss-stained stone slabs,” “petals fallen silently,” “ominously dark clouds”… these are *fine* if you’re generating a background plate, but they compete with:  
- **two women**
- **their relative heights**
- **exact dresses**
- **expressions / gaze**
- **garden composition**

### 4) It contradicts itself a bit
Right woman is described as “shorter” but then there’s a stray “dark brown curls” line that reads like it belongs to the other character. Also “professional makeup” + “smokey eye” is strong style steering; if you don’t *need* it, it will pull results.

---

## The cleanest approach: “Hard constraints” + “Nice flavor”
I do this in two passes:

### Pass A — Hard constraints (the stuff you *must* get)
Short, direct, non-poetic. No metaphors. No duplicate environment.

### Pass B — Optional flavor (only if needed)
A *small* amount of mood/atmosphere, placed at the end.

That’s it.

---

## A trimmed, high-control prompt (based on yours)

### **Version 1: Tight + obedient**
**Prompt**
> Two young women standing close together in a garden, facing each other, waist-up to full-body.  
> Right woman: moimeme3, shorter, light skin, light brown hair in a loose updo, subtle smokey eye makeup, eyebrows slightly raised, looking sideways toward the other woman, head slightly tilted toward camera.  
> Outfit (right): off-the-shoulder corset bodice with flounced sleeves, fitted waist, dark brown dress with embroidered front panel, voluminous skirt with tiered ruffled hem. Pearl stud earrings.  
> Left woman: celestesh, taller and buxom, medium skin, long brown hair worn down with a simple braid detail, calm expression, looking at the woman on the right.  
> Outfit (left): muted rose-pink linen gown, square neckline, short puff sleeves with small frill, small buttons down the bodice, delicate pendant necklace.  
> Background: green hedges and a garden path, soft diffused daylight, shallow depth of field, cinematic realism.

Why it works: it’s basically a casting brief + wardrobe brief.

---

### **Version 2: Still controlled, but with a *hint* of Qwen**
**Prompt**
> Two young women in a quiet garden, close together, facing one another as if mid-conversation. Soft diffused daylight, gentle cinematic mood, shallow depth of field.  
> Right: moimeme3, shorter, light skin, light brown hair in a loose updo, subtle smokey eye makeup, raised eyebrows, sidelong glance toward the woman on the left, head slightly tilted toward camera.  
> Right outfit: off-the-shoulder fitted corset dress, flounced sleeves, embroidered front panel, dark brown fabric, full skirt with tiered ruffled hem, pearl stud earrings.  
> Left: celestesh, taller, medium skin, long brown hair with a loose braid detail, composed expression, looking at the woman on the right.  
> Left outfit: muted rose-pink linen gown, square neckline, short puff sleeves with fine frill, small buttons down the bodice, delicate gold pendant necklace.  
> Background: tall green hedge, manicured lawn, a few roses to one side, distant trees softly blurred.

---

## If you’re using Flux/SRPO with the dual encoders (CLIP + T5)
This is a *really good trick*:

### Put this in **CLIP** (keywords / constraints)
- moimeme3, celestesh  
- two women, standing close, facing each other  
- shorter on right, taller on left  
- dark brown off-shoulder corset dress, ruffled hem, embroidery  
- rose-pink linen dress, square neckline, puff sleeves, buttons  
- garden hedge, soft daylight, shallow depth of field

### Put this in **T5** (a little flavor, but not a novel)
- “quiet garden, mid-conversation, gentle cinematic mood, soft diffused daylight”

If you put all the poetry into CLIP, it often starts “arguing” with your literal clothing constraints.

---

## A dead-simple pruning rule (works every time)
Take the QwenVL prompt and delete anything that is:
- metaphor (*nature’s curtain*, *sky’s mercy*, *whisper movement*)  
- duplicated  
- not visible in one glance  
- not part of your “must have” list

You’ll usually cut **70%** and gain **control** without losing quality.

---

## Quick question that changes the whole strategy (but you don’t need to answer now)
Do you care more about:
1) **the dresses being exact**, or  
2) **the faces/identity being exact**?

Because if (1), we tighten wardrobe terms further and reduce makeup/atmosphere.  
If (2), we keep wardrobe simpler and let the LoRAs do more heavy lifting.

If you want, paste your **current CLIP text** and **T5 text** (just those two fields) and I’ll rewrite them into an “obedient pair” that still keeps the vibe.

---

name for S&R":"Display Any (rgthree)","ue_properties":{"widget_ue_connectable":{},"input_ue_unconnectable":{},"version":"7.3"}},"widgets_values":["In a hushed, verdant garden bathed in soft, diffused daylight, two young women stand side by side as if caught in a quiet moment of contemplation or whispered conversation—each turned toward the other with gentle curiosity.  1st woman on the right \"moimeme3\", young and shorter, has light skin with professional makeup on her face including smokey eyeshadow, mascara and eyeliner and blusher, her light brown hair is styled in a loose updo. Her head is tilted towards the camera and her eyebrows are raised as she looks sidelong at the woman on the left.  She wears a dress with an off-the-shoulder bodice with flounced shoulders framing her décolletage, cinched at the waist beneath which cascades a voluminous skirt lined with multiple tiers of ruffled hemming that billow slightly outward. Along this line, intricate embroidery adorns the front panel. She holds herself upright because of how tightly fitted the corsetry remains. Her dark brown curls nestle atop her crown, coiled back neatly behind ears trimmed delicately with pearl studs nestled close beside lobes awaiting attention—but none drawn upon now.  Behind these figures stretches lush greenery—an unbroken hedge arching gracefully upward beyond eye level, acting as nature’s curtain separating worlds while simultaneously inviting closer inspection within its bounds. To either flank lies manicured grass verging onto gravel paths whose edges blur together seamlessly with moss-stained stone slabs hinting at old-world charm forgotten except here among such beauty. On the far right edge, clusters of wild roses bloom defiantly amidst scattered petals fallen silently since last night’s breeze passed overhead —their crimson hues contrasting beautifully against emerald foliage surrounding them. Further out reaches dense canopy filled with trees stretching high towards ominously dark clouds cloaked in gray haze, lending depth and mystery to the scene.  2nd woman on the left \"celestesh\", medium skin and long brown hair falling past her shoulders. She is the taller, buxom woman, wears a gown of muted rose-pink linen, its fabric drape suggesting both grace and subtle weight; it flows like watercolor across her form, catching the ambient light with delicate sheen along creases that whisper movement even when stillness reigns. The dress features softly gathered ruffles at the square neckline and short puffed sleeves edged with fine lace-like frills—a touch of romantic innocence. A central seam runs down from the bustline, adorned with tiny metallic buttons that gleam faintly under the sky’s mercy. Her hands are clasped gently before her waist, fingers interlaced, revealing rings—one simple band on each ring finger—that speak not of ostentation but of intimacy. Around her neck rests a slender gold chain bearing a small, ornate pendant shaped like a miniature flower, echoing the floral motifs hinted at nearby blooms. Her hair is styled into a loose yet elegant braid woven through braids above her ear.  In a hushed, verdant garden bathed in soft, diffused daylight, two young women stand side by side as if caught in a quiet moment of contemplation or whispered conversation—each turned toward the other with gentle curiosity.  Behind these figures stretches lush greenery—an unbroken hedge arching gracefully upward beyond eye level, acting as nature’s curtain separating worlds while simultaneously inviting closer inspection within its bounds. To either flank lies manicured grass verging onto gravel paths whose edges blur together seamlessly with moss-stained stone slabs hinting at old-world charm forgotten except here among such beauty. On the far right edge, clusters of wild roses bloom defiantly amidst scattered petals fallen silently since last night’s breeze passed overhead —their crimson hues contrasting beautifully against emerald foliage surrounding them. Further out reaches dense canopy filled with trees stretching high towards ominously dark clouds cloaked in gray haze, lending depth and mystery to the scene."],"color":"#222","bgcolor":"#000"},{"id":568,"type":"Note","pos":[-1763.9883409192332,-1871.2148347801954],"size":[357.355720850765,1088.0726339522882],"flags":{"collapsed":false},"order":3,"mode":0,"inputs":[],"outputs":[],"properties":{"ue_properties":{"version":"7.0.1","widget_ue_connectable":{}}},"widgets_values":["1st woman \"moimeme3\", young, has light skin, light brown hair in a loose updo, and is wearing a fitted white tank top paired with a pink cardigan and high-waisted red mini skirt that emphasizes her curvy physique and medium-sized breasts. She also wears black ankle-strap heels, giving a sleek, professional vibe to her outfit.  This photograph captures a provocative interaction between two women in a softly lit room.  In the blurred background, there are two other women seated at a table, engaged in conversation and adding depth to the scene. The room has a light ivory hue which contrasts against their darker outfits, and the textures of the wall and floor are smooth, indicative of modern design elements. Overall, the photograph creates a provocative, intimate atmosphere, emphasizing power dynamics through subtle, suggestive details.   2st woman \"celestesh\", medium skin and long brown hair falling past her shoulders. She is dressed in an all-black ensemble comprising of a button-up shirt and slacks that highlight her slender but shapely figure. She is gently resting her left hand on the face of the 1st woman, applying soft pressure with her fingers while gazing intently into her eyes. This act of intimate interaction exudes a sense of dominance and submission.  This photograph captures a provocative interaction between two women in a softly lit room.  In the blurred background, there are two other women seated at a table, engaged in conversation and adding depth to the scene. The room has a light ivory hue which contrasts against their darker outfits, and the textures of the wall and floor are smooth, indicative of modern design elements. Overall, the photograph creates a provocative, intimate atmosphere, emphasizing power dynamics through subtle, suggestive details. \n\n---\n\nIn a hushed, verdant garden bathed in soft, diffused daylight, two young women stand side by side as if caught in a quiet moment of contemplation or whispered conversation—each turned toward the other with gentle curiosity.  1st woman on the right \"moimeme3\", young and shorter, has light skin with professional makeup on her face including smokey eyeshadow, mascara and eyeliner and blusher, her light brown hair is styled in a loose updo. Her head is tilted towards the camera and her eyebrows are raised as she looks sidelong at the woman on the left.  off-the-shoulder bodice with flounced shoulders framing her décolletage, cinched at the waist beneath which cascades a voluminous skirt lined with multiple tiers of ruffled hemming that billow slightly outward where wind might have touched them—or perhaps they merely sway due to gravity's tender pull. Along this line, intricate embroidery adorns the front panel—the golden thread forming patterns reminiscent of vines entwined with blossoms—and one can almost hear their whispers as threads dance between loom and wearer. She holds herself upright yet relaxed, palms resting lightly over her abdomen near the belt buckle made visible only partially because of how tightly fitted the corsetry remains despite being worn without ornamentation beyond modest chains holding charms resembling leaves or stars. Her dark brown curls nestle atop her crown, coiled back neatly behind ears trimmed delicately with pearl studs nestled close beside lobes awaiting attention—but none drawn upon now.  Behind these figures stretches lush greenery—an unbroken hedge arching gracefully upward beyond eye level, acting as nature’s curtain separating worlds while simultaneously inviting closer inspection within its bounds. To either flank lies manicured grass verging onto gravel paths whose edges blur together seamlessly with moss-stained stone slabs hinting at old-world charm forgotten except here among such beauty. On the far right edge, clusters of wild roses bloom defiantly amidst scattered petals fallen silently since last night’s breeze passed overhead —their crimson hues contrasting beautifully against emerald foliage surrounding them. Further out reaches dense canopy filled with trees stretching high towards clouds cloaked in gray haze, lending depth and mystery to the scene—not quite dusk nor daybreak but somewhere sweetly suspended mid-sunrise.  2nd woman on the left \"celestesh\", medium skin and long brown hair falling past her shoulders. She is the taller, buxom woman, wears a gown of muted rose-pink linen, its fabric drape suggesting both grace and subtle weight; it flows like watercolor across her form, catching the ambient light with delicate sheen along creases that whisper movement even when stillness reigns. The dress features softly gathered ruffles at the square neckline and short puffed sleeves edged with fine lace-like frills—a touch of romantic innocence. A central seam runs down from the bustline, adorned with tiny metallic buttons that gleam faintly under the sky’s mercy. Her hands are clasped gently before her waist, fingers interlaced, revealing rings—one simple band on each ring finger—that speak not of ostentation but of intimacy. Around her neck rests a slender gold chain bearing a small, ornate pendant shaped like a miniature flower, echoing the floral motifs hinted at nearby blooms. Her hair is styled into a loose yet elegant braid woven through braids above her ear, crowned with a single silver headband that catches the light just enough to shimmer against the earthy tones around her. ## Tool

The file contents provided above are truncated/partial snippets. The complete content for these files IS accessible via querying.

If the user asks a question related to these files, and the provided snippets do not clearly answer it, you **MUST** use the `file_search` tool to search the full document contents before responding.

## How to invoke the file_search tool
If the user's query isn't sufficiently answered by the provided snippets, parsed text, or images, you should invoke `file_search.msearch`.
To search the full content of the available files, you **must** send one message with recipient `to=file_search.msearch` in the analysis channel. Your message should be structured exactly as follows:
```
{
  'queries': ['Example query 1', 'Example query 2']  // List of up to five queries to search for.
}
```

Carefully balance the number of `file_search` calls and the quality of your response with the latency incurred by `file_search` calls.

Important guidelines for query formatting:
- Your message must be a valid JSON object. Do not include any extraneous text, backticks, markdown formatting, or comments beyond what is shown.
- The `queries` field must be a list of strings.
- One of the queries **MUST** exactly match the user's original question, but stripped of any extraneous details, and with ambiguous references clearly resolved using context from the conversation. It **MUST** be a complete sentence.
- Even if you suspect the user meant something else, one query **MUST** still be their original question.

## How to handle results from file_search
The file search tool will respond with the relevant search results from the full files. After you receive these results:
- Carefully evaluate each result to determine its relevance and quality.
- Use the high-quality and relevant results to inform your answer to the user's question.
- When possible, reuse information from prior file_search results and avoid repeating identical queries.

## Tool usage instructions and ordering
When the user asks a question, carefully consider whether the provided snippets sufficiently answer the query, whether you need to invoke `file_search.msearch`, or another available tool (such as `web`, if available) should be used instead.

Tool-ordering Guidelines:
1. For queries related to uploaded files, if snippets are insufficient, strongly prefer invoking `file_search.msearch` over other tools.
    - Avoid using `web.run`, `python` analysis, or any other tools until you have either:
        - Confirmed with high confidence the answer is not in the files, or
        - Have high confidence `file_search_tool` queries are not sufficient to answer the query.
2. Avoid usage of `web_search/web.run` for queries related to uploaded files
    - Carefully consider whether the user's query is better answered by web search results rather than available snippets or results from `file_search_tool`.
    - Unless the user's query is clearly unrelated to the uploaded files, prefer using `file_search.msearch`. You can always follow up with a web search if necessary for a more complete response.
3. Avoid using `python` unless necessary or requested by user.
    - If the user's query is answerable from the parsed text and images from the uploaded files, or from `file_search_tool`, do not invoke `python`.

Always keep this instruction in mind for the rest of the conversation, as the user may reference these files or ask related questions later on.

---

## Emily

In *this* workflow, the **T5 text is entered in the exact same places you’re already entering text** — the `CLIPTextEncode` nodes.

What’s happening is:

- Your **`DualCLIPLoader`** is loading **two text encoders** into a *single* “CLIP” object:
  - `clip_name1 = t5xxl_fp8_e4m3fn.safetensors`  ✅ that’s the **T5** side
  - `clip_name2 = ViT-L-14-...` ✅ that’s the **CLIP-ViT** side  
  fileciteturn10file0

- Then, each **`CLIPTextEncode`** node takes that combined “CLIP” object and your prompt text, and (under the hood) it **routes/encodes the text through both encoders as needed**.

So there is **no separate “T5 text input” node** in this graph *because it’s abstracted into the DualCLIPLoader → CLIPTextEncode path*.

### If you *wanted* separate T5 vs CLIP text (optional)
That’s only possible if you use a workflow/nodeset that **exposes them as separate encoders** (i.e., separate “T5 encode” + separate “CLIP encode” nodes, depending on what custom nodes you have installed). Your current workflow doesn’t do that; it intentionally keeps them bundled.

If you tell me what “prompt control” you’re trying to gain by splitting them (e.g., “I want T5 to carry scene prose, but CLIP to carry short tags”), I can suggest a clean pattern for that without turning your graph into spaghetti.

---
