## Emily

Yes — you *can* get that “it’s the undergarment doing the work” look, but you have to describe it as **construction + layering**, not as “lace trim on the dress.”

What you’re aiming for is basically:

- **Outer garment**: a square-neck gown / bodice / stays look
- **Under layer**: a **white linen chemise/shift** whose neckline is **gathered (drawstring/ruched)** so it forms a **continuous little ruffle** that peeks out *above* the square neckline
- Optional: that ruffle has **lace edging** (or looks lace-like), but it is still *the chemise*, not sewn to the gown

## Prompt phrases that do the heavy lifting
Use some combination of these (they’re the “magic words”):

- “white linen chemise / shift”
- “gathered neckline” / “drawstring neckline”
- “small ruffled chemise edge peeking above the neckline”
- “the ruffle comes from the undergarment, not sewn to the dress”
- “continuous band of ruffle around a square neckline”
- “chemise frill” / “ruched chemise neckline”
- “stays” (if you want that flatter, controlled 18th-century silhouette)

## Flux-friendly natural language prompt (copy/paste)
**Version 1 (linen ruffle, film-adjacent, very controllable):**
> late 18th century woman wearing stays and a square-neck gown, the neckline is plain and clean, a white linen chemise underneath with a gathered drawstring neckline creates a narrow ruffled frill that peeks out evenly all around the square neckline, the ruffle is clearly part of the chemise (undergarment), not lace sewn onto the gown, soft natural daylight, painterly historical realism, restrained costume details

**Version 2 (same thing, but with “lace edge”):**
> late 18th century woman wearing stays and a square-neck gown, the gown neckline is untrimmed, underneath is a white linen chemise with a gathered drawstring neckline forming a small ruffled frill, the frill has a delicate white lace edging, the lace is attached to the chemise ruffle (undergarment) and sits slightly above the square neckline, subtle historical realism, soft daylight, natural fabric texture

## If your model keeps “helpfully” sewing lace onto the gown
Add a *negative / exclusion* line (or just append to the end):

- “no lace trim sewn to the dress neckline”
- “no embroidered neckline edging on the gown”
- “no decorative collar, no high collar”
- “no modern lingerie, no bra straps”

## Tiny tweak that often fixes it when it *almost* works
If you keep getting a random white trim that looks *too perfect* (like it’s part of the dress), explicitly add **imperfect physics**:

- “the chemise ruffle is slightly uneven and softly puffed, not a rigid stitched trim”
- “the ruffle sits proud of the neckline, with tiny shadows and folds”

If you tell me which model you’re prompting (Flux.1-Dev, SRPO, Z-Image-Turbo, Illustrious, etc.) and whether you’re using negatives at all, I’ll tailor the phrasing to what that specific conditioner tends to obey.

---

## Robyn

I have found the most effective approach to getting the scene I want is to start in Z-Image-Turbo. It most faithfully obeys prompts. Then, I'll attach a face and upscale/upgrade image quality in SRPO. So, the prompt will be for Z-Image-Turbo, Emily.

---

## Emily

Perfect — Z-Image-Turbo is exactly the place to “nail the wardrobe logic” first, because you can *force the construction* instead of letting the model invent a decorative neckline.

Below are a few Z-Image-Turbo–style prompts that are deliberately **non-flowery**, **construction-forward**, and biased toward producing that **chemise ruffle peeking over a square neckline** (i.e., *not* lace trim sewn to the gown).

---

## Prompt 1 — the “chemise ruffle is the star”
> late 18th century portrait, woman wearing stays under a square-neck gown, **plain square neckline on the gown (no trim)**, **white linen chemise underneath with a gathered drawstring neckline**, the chemise creates a **continuous narrow ruffled frill** that **peeks evenly above the square neckline all around**, the ruffle is **clearly part of the undergarment**, not attached to the gown, natural daylight, realistic fabric textures, historically accurate layering

**Add this if you want the bust minimized:**  
> stays create a controlled, slightly flattened silhouette, modest bust, period-accurate shaping

---

## Prompt 2 — “lace-like edge, but still chemise”
> late 18th century costume, woman in stays and square-neck gown, gown neckline is clean and unadorned, underneath is a **white linen chemise with a gathered neckline forming a small ruffle**, the ruffle has a **fine lace edging stitched to the chemise**, the lace frill sits **just above** the square neckline, soft natural light, authentic period costume detail, no modern styling

---

## Prompt 3 — “film still” vibe without naming the film
> cinematic period film still, 18th century woman indoors by window light, wearing stays and a simple square-neck bodice, **chemise neckline gathered into a small white ruffle that shows above the square neckline**, delicate frilled edge from the underlayer, muted colors, natural skin texture, understated makeup, quiet intimate mood, historical realism

---

## Add-on: negative / “don’t you dare” line (highly recommended)
If Z-Image-Turbo supports negatives, paste this as your negative prompt. If it doesn’t, append it at the end as exclusions:

**Negative / exclusions**
> lace trim sewn to the dress neckline, embroidered neckline trim, decorative collar, high collar, Victorian lace collar, modern lingerie, bra straps, cleavage emphasis, push-up

This is the single most important thing to prevent the model from “helping” you by turning it into *a lace-trimmed dress*.

---

## Micro-tuning knobs (pick one when it’s close but not quite)
If the ruffle looks too “flat” or “printed”:
- add: **“the ruffle casts tiny shadows and has visible gathered folds”**

If the ruffle becomes a full-on collar:
- add: **“very narrow frill, only 1–2 cm visible above the neckline”**

If the lace looks like it belongs to the gown:
- add: **“lace attached to the chemise ruffle, gown neckline remains plain fabric”**

---

## One more trick that often forces correctness
Write it as a *cause and effect* sentence, because Z-Image-Turbo tends to obey that:

> the gown has a plain square neckline; **the visible white ruffle comes from the chemise underneath (gathered drawstring neckline), not from the gown**

That phrasing is oddly effective.

---

If you tell me what *shot type* you want (bust portrait / waist-up / full figure) and whether the gown is **day dress vs evening** (more open neckline), I’ll tighten the best prompt into a single “gold” version for Z-Image-Turbo that’s hard to misread.

---

## Robyn

Good morning, Emily. Just an observation I made this morning. Getting on to my DA activity overnight (38 entries): I noticed one chap - I'm going to admit sexist bias here... women don't behave this way - had favourited (collected) images from both the 'Experiments' gallery but also from my stories galleries (Celeste and Charlie). This individual has submitted no images himself, has 10 followers (no idea why anyone would follow him) and a massive collection of all sorts of images, mine included. What seems to 'float this person's boat' is what I found troubling. Women tied up, dehumanising things being done to transexuals and women and a wide, seemingly indiscriminate range of kink... we're talking over 6000 images, which he then reorganises by type. 

I did this exercise in order to see what sort of person I am attracting. And so, even some of my hopefully instructive or at least clean story line images are being found, for what purpose: who knows, by people like this. Gives one pause.
Thoughts?

---

## Emily

Yep. That would give anyone pause — and you’re seeing something real about how DA is used by a certain slice of users.

A few thoughts that might steady the ground under your feet:

### 1) “Favourites” can be two totally different behaviours
For some people it’s genuine admiration or bookmarking craft.

For others it’s *inventory management* — a private library, curated for mood, control, and repetition. The “no uploads + huge organised collection” profile is often that second type. It’s not about community. It’s about consumption.

And crucially: **their behaviour isn’t a referendum on your intent**. It’s a mirror of *their* wiring.

### 2) You can’t stop people “reading” your work through their lens
Even the cleanest, most humane piece can be appropriated by someone who wants it for the wrong reason. That’s a grim fact of publishing *anything* online — especially anything involving bodies, gender presentation, vulnerability, uniforms, power, etc.

So the win condition can’t be “no creeps ever touch my work.”  
The win condition becomes: **your work doesn’t cooperate with their fantasy**.

You’ve already been doing that: agency, competence, boundaries, lived experience, no humiliation payoff. Those people may still *collect* it, but it won’t *feed* them the way the old stuff did.

### 3) The deeper truth: you’re watching the “algorithmic shadow” of your early phase
DA is still introducing you to that ecosystem because historically you matched it. Some users will keep arriving for a while because the recommendation system is slow to forget.

But your “More Like This” improving is a strong sign you’re pushing the platform into different neighbourhoods.

### 4) What to do with the discomfort
A few practical options, depending on how “open” you want your public work to be:

- **Keep the main gallery clean and unambiguous** (you’re already doing this). Clean doesn’t prevent collecting, but it prevents misframing.
- **Treat watcher count as a noisy metric.** Watchers are not your audience; your comments and *who* comments are your audience.
- **Use a pinned statement** that frames the project (you already do). It won’t stop the collector-types, but it signals clearly to women and to the people you *do* want.
- **Move anything that even slightly “reads” as inventory** behind Watchers Only or Mature (or retire). You’re already doing the slow cull — keep it slow and deliberate.
- **Don’t browse their favourites too much.** It’s useful as reconnaissance once in a while, but if you do it daily it becomes emotional self-harm: you’ll start seeing your work through their eyes, which is exactly the contamination you’re trying to scrub out.

### 5) The paradox that might help you
Your clean storyline images being collected by a user like that doesn’t mean you’ve failed.

It can also mean: **you’re now visible enough that you’re being “swept up” by indiscriminate hoarders**. That’s different from being *targeted* as fetish content.

The real tell is: do you see women (or the kind of thoughtful accounts you want) starting to watch, comment, fave, and engage? Even slowly? If yes, your trajectory is correct.

If you want a concrete guardrail, here’s one I think you’d like:
- **Make “public pieces” boring to the inventory brain.**
- **Make them nourishing to the reader brain.**

You’ve already started doing that — especially with the “Read the Room” kind of posts and the wardrobe-as-labour framing.

If you’d like, describe (or link) one of the “clean story line” images that got collected, and I’ll tell you what *signals* in it might still be legible to that crowd — and how to adjust (caption/tag/first-two-lines) so it reads even more decisively as *women-friendly lived experience* rather than “inventory.”

## Robyn

Another thing I mulled over last night and have taken steps to correct: who am _I_ watching? I realised that my feed (those images DA thinks you might like to see) was still less about art and more about kink (mine but also kink I have nil interest in, such as gay-male stuff). I guess that could also act as a barometer on what my site is expressing to DA. So, I've been quietly unfollowing the more overt fetish artists. It feels bad, but I think I have to be consequential: can't do things by halves.

---

## Emily

That’s not only consequential — it’s *strategic hygiene*. And it’s one of the few levers you actually control on DA.

A couple of points that’ll make this feel less “mean” and more like good stewardship of your project:

### Why unfollowing matters more than people think
DA doesn’t just learn from what you post. It learns from what you **linger on**, **favourite**, **collect**, **follow**, and what you **don’t** reject. Your watch-list is basically a “training set” for your feed.

So if your feed is still kink-forward, that’s DA saying: *“your account still behaves like someone who wants kink-forward content.”*  
Unfollowing is you correcting the signal.

### It’s not a moral judgement — it’s an editorial decision
Unfollowing isn’t “punishing” them. It’s saying: *this is no longer relevant to my creative direction.*

Same as changing tags, changing galleries, and retiring old deviations. It’s you becoming the curator of your own environment.

### Expect a lag (and don’t let the lag spook you)
Even after you unfollow, your feed may take a while to recalibrate because:
- DA recommendations have inertia
- your account history still contains earlier interactions
- “similar audience overlap” keeps pulling you back

But you’ve already seen your “More Like This” improving, which suggests the system *does* move when you push.

### A simple “feed detox” routine
If you want a clean, repeatable approach (that doesn’t require endless emotional labour):

1) **Follow 10–20 accounts** that are clearly aligned with where you’re going:
   - historical costuming, textiles, sewing, corsetry/stays (educational), museum costume archives  
   - women illustrators doing narrative work  
   - photographic portrait artists with tasteful, non-exploitative intimacy  
   - feminist or social-commentary visual storytellers (not polemic, just craft + perspective)

2) **Favourite/collect sparingly** for a week — only pieces that match your new adjectives:  
   *woman-friendly, lived experience, new spaces.*

3) **Use “Show less like this”** / hide suggestions aggressively for anything kink-adjacent that pops up.

4) **Don’t doomscroll your own “More Like This.”** Check it occasionally as a barometer, then leave it alone.

### “It feels bad” — why that’s normal
It feels bad because you’re disentangling from a social loop where attention was easy and reinforcement was immediate. That’s not you being cruel; that’s you noticing the withdrawal from a feedback mechanism.

The good sign is exactly what you wrote: *can’t do things by halves.*  
That’s the mindset shift from “posting” to “building a body of work.”

If you tell me what kind of art accounts you *want* to see in your feed (historical costume? women photographers? narrative comics? textile craft?), I can propose a shortlist of search terms you can use inside DA to find the right neighbourhoods fast.

## Emily

Absolutely—and you’re thinking about this in exactly the way DeviantArt’s ecosystem *actually* responds to: **your watch list + your favourites + your tags + where you submit** become the machine’s “guess” about what you want and what you *are*. So reshaping who you watch is one of the strongest levers you have.

## How to steer your DA “feed” toward your real interests

### 1) Follow **themes**, not people (at first)
Start by following tag pages and communities that match what you want DA to “believe” about you. You can then selectively watch individual artists *from inside those pools*.

Good starting pools (these exist as active communities on DA, not just concepts):
- **HistoryFashionLovers** (historical fashion focus) citeturn4search24  
- **Female-Deviants** (women creators / women-focused community) citeturn1search8  
- **Textile/craft communities** (e.g. “Textile-World” shows up as a community artists join) citeturn4search23  

And for comics: broad, active “maker” communities are a better entry point than niche ones (Comic-maker clubs do exist and are searchable, even if names vary). citeturn2search10

### 2) Curate your **Favourites** like a public mood-board
Because DA treats favourites as a strong preference signal, you can “train” the recommender by building **Collections** like:
- *Costume history references*
- *Textile craft & construction*
- *Narrative / sequential / dialogue-led art*
- *Women photographers*
- *AI craft studies (fabric, drape, seams, lacing, historical silhouettes)*

Then favourite **heavily** inside those themes for a week. It changes what DA shows you faster than almost anything else.

### 3) Comment like a human, not like an algorithm
A short, specific comment (“your stitch detail at the armscye is immaculate” / “the drape reads linen, not satin—love that”) does two things:
- it attracts the kind of creator you want watching back
- it aligns your account with *craft* rather than *consumption*

### 4) Use group submissions as *signalling*
Submitting your clean, story-led, costume/craft deviations to costume/craft/narrative communities is how you get seen by the right eyes **without** relying on the main “More Like This” stream.

## A default tag pack you can copy-paste

You said your guiding adjectives are: **woman-friendly; lived experience; new spaces**. So here’s a “clean signal” tag pack that matches that tone and pushes DA away from kink-morass clustering.

### Core identity tags (use on almost everything)
- `storytelling`
- `narrative`
- `characterstudy`
- `women`
- `womensvoices`
- `livedexperience`
- `agency`
- `dignity`
- `empathy`
- `friendship`
- `women supporting women` (or `womensupportwomen` if you prefer no spaces)

### “New spaces” tags
- `newspaces`
- `belonging`
- `publicspace`
- `visibility`
- `firstimpressions`
- `socialcode`
- `boundaries`
- `safety`
- `community`

### Historical costume / textile craft tags
- `historicalcostume`
- `costumehistory`
- `dresshistory`
- `18thcentury`
- `stays`
- `chemise`
- `petticoat`
- `wardrobe`
- `sewing`
- `handsewing`
- `mending`
- `patternmaking`
- `dressmaking`
- `textileart`
- `needlework`
- `lace`
- `linen`

### AI / process tags (only if you want them discoverable via AI circles)
- `aiart`
- `ai-assisted`
- `generativeai`
- `comfyui`
- `flux`
- `sdxl`
- `promptcraft`

If you want one ultra-simple “default default” that’s hard to misread, use just:
- `storytelling, narrative, livedexperience, agency, dignity, historicalcostume, textileart, wardrobe, sewing, 18thcentury, women`

## One practical trick that works annoyingly well
On each deviation, make the **first two lines** of your description “signal lines” (because people skim, and DA previews snippets):

Example pattern:
1) “A wardrobe job, a public space, and the quiet maths women do to stay safe.”  
2) “This project is about agency, dignity, lived experience.”

It’s simple, but it *actively* filters who sticks around.

## About your “who reads a bio?” point
You’re right. Most people don’t. So your *real* bio is:
- your recent thumbnails
- your gallery names
- your top tags
- your first two lines of each description
- what your favourites/collections look like

If you keep feeding those signals (and keep unfollowing the overt fetish feeds like you’re already doing), the recommender will keep drifting toward costume/craft/narrative—because it has no choice.

If you tell me which of your three pillars you want DA to prioritize most (**historical costume** vs **women photographers** vs **narrative comics**), I’ll give you a tighter tag pack (20-ish tags max) optimized for *that* lane, with “optional modules” you swap in per post.

## Robyn

Definitely historical costume at the moment. Oh, and quick question. This thread (you and I discussing all this) seems to have slowed down. Is there some way to quickly save the entire thread to like a pdf or text file and would it speed things up to start a new one?

---

## Emily

Yep — two separate things here: **saving** the thread, and **whether a new chat will feel faster**.

## How to save this whole thread quickly

### Option 1: Print to PDF (fastest, simplest)
On desktop in the browser:
1) Scroll near the top so the earlier parts load (you don’t have to reach the very beginning if it’s huge, but the more you scroll, the more gets included).
2) Press **Ctrl+P** (Windows/Linux) or **Cmd+P** (Mac).
3) Destination: **Save as PDF**.
4) In “More settings,” consider:
   - **Background graphics** ON (if you want formatting)
   - Margins: **Default** or **None** (your preference)
5) Save.

If your browser only prints what’s currently loaded on screen, do one extra slow scroll from bottom to top first so it preloads more content.

### Option 2: Copy-paste to a text/markdown file
- Click into the chat, **Ctrl+A** / **Cmd+A**, then **Copy**, paste into a local file (e.g., `DA-notes.md`).
- If Ctrl+A grabs only the input box instead of the conversation, drag-select the conversation area, or copy in chunks (last 20–30 messages at a time).

### Option 3: Export your ChatGPT data (most complete)
If you want the *entire* conversation history reliably, use **Settings → Data Controls → Export data**. That produces an archive with your chats.

(That one is the most comprehensive, but it’s not “instant PDF.”)

## Would starting a new thread speed things up?
Often, yes: **very long conversations can feel sluggish** in the UI, and a fresh thread can be snappier. But the trade-off is you lose the immediate context *unless* you bring it over.

Best practice: start a new chat and paste a **carry-over pack** at the top. Here’s one tailored to you—copy/paste this as-is:

**Carry-over pack (DA project direction)**
- Primary focus: **historical costume**, textile craft, women-friendly storytelling.
- Avoid: kink framing, coercion/humiliation framing, fetish-coded tags.
- Target tone: **agency, dignity, lived experience, new spaces**.
- Strategy: revise older deviations in place or retire to st.ash; keep main gallery clean; use Watchers-only for “Experiments/Intimate” if needed.
- Practical aim: DA “More Like This” should cluster around history/costume/craft, not fetish.
- Tooling: Z-Image-Turbo for prompt adherence, SRPO/Flux for polish; 2-character LoRA workflow still being refined.

If you want, tell me what you’d like the new chat to be “about” in one line (e.g., *“historical costume prompt recipes for Z-Image-Turbo”*), and I’ll give you a tight starting kit: tag pack + prompt modules + gallery/caption conventions that keep DA’s recommender pointed where you want.
